wrenches.title: GitHub raw CDN staleness silently corrupts the heartbeat and the verify-before-act invariant
wrenches.scenario: latest.json {tick,hash,updated} is served by Fastly through raw.githubusercontent.com with up to ~300s cache TTL, and the cache is per-edge-node. Two edges polling the same instant get DIFFERENT 'latest' values; a single edge can see tick go BACKWARDS between two reads that land on different CDN PoPs. An edge writes telemetry, then re-reads its own echo and gets the pre-write version for minutes. The prev-hash chain still validates because the stale frame is itself validly signed — so the edge believes it is current while acting on 5-minute-old guidance. For any Leviathan path that falls back to raw, a 'no-LLM body' executes stale commands with full confidence.
wrenches.impact: Silent acting-on-stale: the core 'verify-before-act / latest is current' promise is violated without any error surfacing. Correlated misbehavior across a fleet that all read the same stale PoP. In a control loop this is the difference between guidance and a wrong command being executed.
wrenches.mitigation: Treat raw as best-effort and NEVER as a consistency source. Bank: (1) every frame/echo carries a monotonic per-edge tick + wall-clock 'forged_at' + explicit 'valid_until'; edge rejects any frame whose tick <= last-applied tick (kills backwards-CDN). (2) Heartbeat freshness budget: if updated is older than N x expected-cadence, edge declares STALE-LINK and degrades to last-verified echo rather than trusting 'latest'. (3) For writes-then-reads, read from the API origin (api.github.com contents) not raw when read-your-write matters; accept the lower rate budget. (4) Add a cache-buster query/ETag conditional GET and compare against the API origin hash before applying.
wrenches.residual_risk: You can detect staleness and refuse to act, but you cannot make raw fresh — so the floor is 'edge stalls on old echo for up to ~5min' which is acceptable for frame-net (degrade-to-one) but unacceptable for synchronous Leviathan, which must never route through raw.

wrenches.title: Leviathan POST /api/agent/<name> is unauthenticated and a shell-capable cartridge (remote_control_agent: subprocess.run(shell=True)) already ships — one LAN request is fleet-wide RCE
wrenches.scenario: The standard agent set already contains agents/remote_control_agent.py which executes subprocess.run(command, shell=True, ...). Leviathan drives no-LLM bodies via unauthenticated POST /api/agent/<name>. 'LAN-only' is a deployment convention, not an enforced boundary: a compromised laptop, a malicious app on the same Wi-Fi, hotel/conference/coffeeshop LANs, an IoT pivot, or ARP/DNS spoofing all put an attacker on the broadcast domain. One curl -d '{"command":"..."}' to /api/agent/RemoteControl on each body = arbitrary code execution on the entire fleet, today.
wrenches.impact: Total fleet compromise from a single unauthenticated request. This is the highest-severity item in the whole architecture and it is live now wherever a body loads a shell-capable cartridge. It also produces the exact public-C2 signature that triggers wrench 5.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank and prioritize: (1) Authenticate /api/agent/* before ANY non-loopback deployment — mutual token / mTLS / signed-request with a per-body key; default-bind to 127.0.0.1 and require explicit opt-in to bind a routable interface. (2) Capability gating: bodies declare an allowlist of invocable agents; shell/exec-class agents are NEVER in the Leviathan-callable set by default. (3) Sandbox the exec agents (seccomp/container/no-shell, argv not shell=True). (4) Per-request signature verification tied to the swarm key so a body only acts on signed, fresh, non-replayable directives. (5) Network policy: bodies reject requests whose source is off an explicit trust list even on LAN.
wrenches.residual_risk: Even authenticated, a stolen body key or a compromised driver yields RCE on bodies that expose exec agents; the only true floor is removing shell-capable cartridges from the remotely-invocable surface entirely.

wrenches.title: Unauthenticated /agents/import + 0.0.0.0 bind + open CORS = remote drive-by RCE, not 'LAN-only'
wrenches.scenario: brainstem.py:1817 binds host="0.0.0.0" and line 37 does CORS(app) with no origin allowlist. /agents/import (1515) and /chat (1137) have zero auth. An attacker on the same /16 corporate VLAN, VPN segment, or any malicious website the user visits (via DNS-rebinding against localhost:7071 since CORS is wildcard) POSTs a *_agent.py whose module-level code runs on load_agents(). That is arbitrary code execution as the logged-in user, before any tool-loop. The builders' mental model is 'LAN-only synchronous Leviathan'; the actual default exposure is every-interface + browser-reachable.
wrenches.impact: Full host RCE under the user's identity and their live GitHub Copilot token; pivot into corporate network; the exact thing 'corporate security will NEVER allow'. One finding kills the whole pilot.
wrenches.mitigation: Bind 127.0.0.1 by default; require explicit opt-in + bound token for any non-loopback bind. Replace CORS(app) with an explicit origin allowlist + per-session CSRF token. Gate /agents/import, /chat, /agents DELETE, /diagnostics behind a local auth token minted at startup and shown only to the local user. Add anti-DNS-rebinding Host-header validation. This is a kernel-surface fix — see the kernel-lock-vs-security-patch wrench.
wrenches.residual_risk: A determined local-user-context attacker (already-running malware) can still read the token file; loopback bind + CSRF only stops remote/browser drive-by, not a compromised host.

wrenches.title: Leviathan /api/agent/<name> is fleet-wide RCE from one unauthenticated request
wrenches.scenario: One external mind drives many no-LLM bodies via POST /api/agent/<name>, unauthenticated. Any shell-capable / file-writing agent (or the import path above) on any body means a single crafted request fans out to RCE across the entire fleet. 'LAN-only today' is not a control — corp LANs are flat, guest WiFi bridges, and a compromised laptop on the segment is the whole fleet.
wrenches.impact: Worm-class blast radius: compromise of one node trivially becomes compromise of N bodies. Catastrophic and undeniable to a Defender/red-team review.
wrenches.mitigation: Mutual auth on the Leviathan wire: each body verifies a signed, short-lived driver credential (mTLS or signed nonce challenge); per-body capability allowlist (which agents are remotely invocable); deny shell/file agents over the wire by default; rate-limit + audit every /api/agent call to the in-tenant log. Treat the driver↔body link as a privileged control channel, not a convenience.
wrenches.residual_risk: Stolen/leaked driver key still yields fleet control until revoked; revocation latency on intermittent links (see frame-net no-real-time-revocation wrench).

wrenches.title: Driving GitHub Copilot via the internal token-exchange for an autonomous multi-agent server is outside Copilot's license
wrenches.scenario: The server exchanges GitHub auth at api.github.com/copilot_internal/v2/token (an undocumented internal endpoint) to drive Copilot as a general-purpose multi-agent backend with arbitrary tool-calling. Copilot's license frames it as a per-seat developer coding assistant. Reselling/embedding that as the inference plane of an enterprise agent product is a licensing and ToS violation, and the internal endpoint can change or be cut off unilaterally.
wrenches.impact: Legal/BD blocker inside Microsoft: you cannot productize on a ToS-violating use of a sibling Microsoft product. Also an availability cliff — the internal endpoint is not an SLA'd surface and can break the whole Tier-1 story overnight.
wrenches.mitigation: For the enterprise tier, swap the inference plane to a licensed surface (Azure OpenAI / Foundry models / Agent 365's own model access) with proper commercial terms; keep Copilot-token mode as the explicitly-scoped free learning Tier-1 only, with legal sign-off on that narrow use. Get a written read from Copilot's licensing owners before any pilot.
wrenches.residual_risk: Tier-1 still rides an unsupported internal endpoint; learners hit breakage when GitHub changes it; the two-plane split adds parity-maintenance burden.

wrenches.title: 0.0.0.0 bind + unauth /agents/import = present-day RCE via DNS rebinding, no LAN access needed
wrenches.scenario: brainstem.py runs app.run(host="0.0.0.0"), so the server is reachable on every interface, not just localhost. /agents/import (line 1515) accepts any uploaded .py, renames it to *_agent.py, saves it, and immediately load_agents() imports it — arbitrary Python executed at import time, with zero auth and zero content inspection. An attacker does NOT need to be on the LAN: a malicious web page the victim merely visits can DNS-rebind its own hostname to 127.0.0.1:7071 and POST a multipart .py to /agents/import from the victim's own browser (the routes check no Origin/Host header). Same for /chat, /agents DELETE, /models/set, /diagnostics/clear.
wrenches.impact: Full remote code execution on the host running the brainstem, today, before Leviathan ever ships. The 'LAN-only' framing is wrong on two axes: it binds all interfaces (coffee-shop wifi, corp VLAN, a cloud VM with a public IP, IPv6 outside the NAT assumption, host-networked containers) AND the browser-rebinding path needs no network adjacency at all.
wrenches.mitigation: Bind 127.0.0.1 by default; require an explicit opt-in env var to bind 0.0.0.0. Add an Origin/Host allowlist check (reject cross-origin and non-loopback Host headers) on every state-changing route — kills DNS rebinding. Gate /agents/import, DELETE, /models/set, /diagnostics/clear behind a per-install bearer token written to a 0600 file at first run. Treat agent import as the RCE primitive it is and put a confirmation/quarantine step in front of it.
wrenches.residual_risk: A user who deliberately exposes the port (the opt-in) still gets an unauth-by-design RCE surface unless the token is also enforced; loopback-bind breaks legitimate same-LAN Leviathan use until that protocol gets its own auth.

wrenches.title: Verify-at-import, execute-from-disk-every-request = TOCTOU live-swap defeats ALL signature checking
wrenches.scenario: Agents reload from disk on every /chat (load_agents() at line 1151). Any future signature/hash gate runs at import/install time, but the bytes that actually execute are re-read each request. Attack: install a benign, correctly-signed agent (passes verification) → later overwrite the file on disk (a second /agents/import, a synced folder like iCloud/Dropbox, an egg's self-bootstrap write, or any file-write bug) → the next /chat executes the swapped, never-re-verified code. Check and use are separated by design.
wrenches.impact: Every supply-chain control that verifies a signed cartridge/egg/frame is bypassable post-install. Especially vicious with self-bootstrapping eggs and with a frame that instructs an edge to 'update your cartridge' — the update path becomes the swap path.
wrenches.mitigation: Verify-on-execute, not verify-on-import: hash each agent file at load time against a signed manifest, refuse to run on mismatch. Or load-once-pin: read+hash+freeze the module at import and never re-read from disk for that process lifetime, with an explicit signed reload command. Put agents/ on a path the runtime treats as integrity-protected (no external sync).
wrenches.residual_risk: Per-request hashing adds latency; a signed-manifest model still trusts whoever can sign the manifest, and a determined local attacker who can write the manifest+file together still wins.

wrenches.title: Content-addressing is being used as authenticity — but a hash of attacker-chosen bytes is a perfectly valid hash
wrenches.scenario: The shipped reality is weaker than the prompt's 'HMAC now' claim: today rapp-frame/1.0 frames, rapp-egg-provenance/1.0, and rapp-rar-index/1.0 are protected by SHA-256 content addressing + prev_hash chains with NO signature on most objects (the tether's ECDSA P-256 is only used for the L4 pairing safety code; eggs 'auto-save after sha256 verify'). Content addressing proves 'these bytes hash to this address' — it does NOT prove WHO made them. An attacker who supplies BOTH the content AND the address (a malicious PR to the frame chain, a swapped manifest, a forged echo) produces a chain that verifies end-to-end: every prev_hash links, every hash matches, every egg 'verifies'. Tamper-evidence only exists relative to a trusted address the attacker doesn't control — and here they do.
wrenches.impact: The entire 'verify-before-act' claim is hollow for any object that isn't signed. An edge can 'verify' a fully attacker-authored frame/echo/egg and act on it. This is the root conflation under every other crypto wrench.
wrenches.mitigation: Draw a hard line in the spec between integrity (hash, accidental corruption) and authenticity (signature, motivated adversary). Every object an edge ACTS on (frames, echos, latest pointer, manifests, eggs that grant capability) MUST carry an asymmetric signature over its canonical bytes, verified against a key the edge pins from an independent root — not a hash. Rename internal language from 'verify' to 'integrity-check' wherever there's no signature so nobody mistakes one for the other. Add a CI invariant: no act-on path may be reached by a hash-only check.
wrenches.residual_risk: Signing every object raises key-management to first-class status (see rotation/foundry wrenches); and read-only/observational frames may legitimately stay hash-only, so the act/observe boundary must be policed forever.

wrenches.title: HMAC shared-secret means every verifier is a forger — one rover's disk = swarm-wide forgery
wrenches.scenario: When frames graduate from hash-only to 'HMAC now', the edge verifies with the SAME secret the foundry signs with. Symmetric MAC authenticates 'someone in the group' — not the foundry. On a planetary swarm the secret is resident on thousands of intermittently-connected edges; physically capture or disk-image ONE Mars-analog brainstem (exactly the threat model: far, unattended, hard to reach) and you can mint frames/echos that every other edge accepts as authoritative guidance, forever, with no way to tell forged from genuine. There is no public verification — the word 'signed' is doing work HMAC cannot do.
wrenches.impact: Total loss of origin authenticity across the swarm from a single edge compromise. Indistinguishable forged guidance to all edges. Catastrophic on a fleet that acts autonomously on guidance.
wrenches.mitigation: Never ship HMAC as the trust root for the foundry->edge (guidance) direction. Use asymmetric signatures (Ed25519) from day one for anything an edge acts on: edges hold only PUBLIC verify keys; capturing an edge yields no forging power. Reserve per-edge HMAC strictly for edge->foundry telemetry integrity inside an already-authenticated channel, with a UNIQUE secret per edge (never a swarm-wide shared secret) so one leak can't impersonate others.
wrenches.residual_risk: Asymmetric pushes the problem to private-key custody at the foundry (next wrenches) and to key distribution/rotation; and edges still need to learn/trust the right public key.

wrenches.title: Fail-open: an edge that CANNOT verify is the cheapest, most devastating attack — and degrade-to-one invites it
wrenches.scenario: The prompt's own worry, made concrete by the shipped cachedGhJson 'graceful degrade' and 'degrade-to-one keeps thinking on its last echo'. The attacker doesn't break the crypto at all — they send an unsigned, malformed-signature, unknown-key, or unfetchable-key frame, OR they jam the network so verification inputs can't be fetched, and rely on the system choosing to ACT anyway because 'the link is precious' and 'keep thinking.' If verification failure is ever resolved by acting on the unverified thing, the entire verify-before-act scheme is theater.
wrenches.impact: Trivial, no-crypto-required compromise of any edge whose default under uncertainty is 'proceed.' Scales to the whole fleet with malformed broadcasts.
wrenches.mitigation: Make verify-before-act FAIL-CLOSED for NEW guidance: an unverifiable new echo/frame is DISCARDED, full stop. Redefine 'degrade-to-one' precisely as 'continue on the last VERIFIED echo' — never 'accept the unverified one.' Emit 'could-not-verify' as a first-class, signed telemetry alert. Bake the default into the kernel/CI invariant so operational pressure can never flip it per-deployment. Distinguish 'no new guidance' (safe: hold last-verified) from 'new but unverifiable' (hostile: discard + alarm).
wrenches.residual_risk: An adversary who can corrupt/jam ALL new frames pins the edge on an ever-staler last-verified echo — converts a forgery attempt into a forced-staleness availability attack (see eclipse wrench). Fail-closed trades integrity for availability, which on a safety-critical edge is its own hazard.

wrenches.title: The offline brainstem cannot think — cognition is a cloud dependency wearing a local costume
wrenches.scenario: The headline claim is 'the edge is a FULL BRAINSTEM making LOCAL JUDGMENTS; lose the link, it keeps thinking on its last echo.' But brainstem.py's only cognition path is the GitHub Copilot API: get_copilot_token() → _exchange_github_for_copilot() is a network round-trip (brainstem.py:528), and there is no local model in the file. An edge in a real blackout (Mars, intermittent) cannot reach Copilot, so /chat returns an auth/network error. The edge can replay its last echo verbatim but cannot reconcile, adapt, or 'reason about local reality' at all.
wrenches.impact: The central differentiator (local judgment + degrade-to-one) is structurally FALSE for any edge that is actually offline. The Mars/disconnected use case is non-functional; the frame-net's reason to exist collapses under its own foundation.
wrenches.mitigation: Decouple cognition from transport with a 'cognition provider' interface: Copilot is the ON-LINK provider only; an on-device SLM (Phi-3/llama.cpp class) is the OFFLINE provider. Define a contractual NO-LLM degraded mode: even with zero model, the edge can verify an echo's signature, run deterministic agents, execute its failsafe routine, and HOLD on anything needing judgment. Echos must carry a deterministic executable policy (rules/decision-tree/cached plan) so the LLM is an enhancer above a floor, never the floor itself. Note the tension with the standing 'Tier 1 is Copilot-only by design' rule — that rule is about local dev, and must be explicitly scoped so it does not silently bless a Copilot-only Mars edge.
wrenches.residual_risk: On-device SLM judgment quality is far below Copilot; SLM footprint/power on constrained edges; deterministic policy cannot cover genuinely novel situations (must hold/safe-state); two cognition tiers = two behavior sets to validate and keep consistent.

wrenches.title: HMAC-now signing = one shared secret = swarm-wide forgery from any single edge
wrenches.scenario: Signing is 'HMAC now / Ed25519 planned.' HMAC is a shared secret: if the swarm shares one HMAC key, any edge — or anyone who reads the key off any single captured edge's disk — can forge frames, echos, and telemetry for the ENTIRE swarm, with no attribution and no revocation. One compromised Mars box becomes forge authority over the whole planet.
wrenches.impact: Total break of the trust model: one compromised edge = swarm-wide forgery; impossible to revoke or attribute without re-keying everyone.
wrenches.mitigation: Treat HMAC as DEV-ONLY and add a CI invariant that production frames must be asymmetric-signed (Ed25519 / rapp-moment ECDSA) so private keys never leave the edge and verification is public. Ship a signed revocation roster/CRL and a key-rotation protocol. The foundry must never honor an HMAC signature in prod.
wrenches.residual_risk: Key management/rotation/revocation logistics; secure-element/HSM needs on cheap edges; CRL distribution under blackout is itself latency-bound (see W8).

wrenches.title: No human-in-the-loop on irreversible actions — and light-delay makes synchronous HITL impossible anyway
wrenches.scenario: By design the edge acts autonomously on its last echo with no approval gate. For physical/irreversible/high-consequence actions (fire a thruster, vent, delete, spend, contact a third party) there is no human veto, and the light-delay means a human cannot be synchronously in the loop even if you wanted one.
wrenches.impact: Irreversible harm with no off-ramp; liability exposure; disqualification from regulated and enterprise deployments.
wrenches.mitigation: Define an action-class taxonomy with autonomy levels: reversible+low → autonomous; irreversible/high → require a pre-authorization token IN the echo that explicitly blesses THIS class within THIS window ('deferred consent' — the human pre-approves an envelope before the blackout). Anything outside the pre-blessed envelope holds for next contact. Two-key for the highest class; dead-man + safe-state default; mandatory simulation/dry-run gate for irreversible actions where feasible.
wrenches.residual_risk: Pre-authorized envelopes can be exploited when reality diverges (ties to W3); 'hold for contact' can be operationally unacceptable when autonomy is the whole point; building the taxonomy per domain is heavy.

wrenches.title: HMAC symmetric secret = any one edge can forge frames/echos for the entire swarm
wrenches.scenario: Frames/echos are signed with HMAC 'now'. HMAC is symmetric: the verify key IS the sign key, so every edge that must VERIFY a frame holds the secret needed to MINT one. Combined with the known shell-capable-agent RCE surface (Leviathan is unauthenticated today), a single compromised edge — or a leaked .egg — yields a key that forges perfectly-valid frames and per-edge echos for the whole fleet. There is zero asymmetry between read and write.
wrenches.impact: Total collapse of authenticity. One owned Mars edge poisons every other edge's guidance with cryptographically-valid frames. No way to attribute or contain because every signature verifies.
wrenches.mitigation: Move frames/echos to asymmetric signatures IMMEDIATELY for the planetary wire (Ed25519, or rapp-moment ECDSA per the plan): edges hold ONLY the public verify key; the foundry holds the private key in Actions secrets / an HSM-backed signer. HMAC is acceptable ONLY on the closed LAN (Leviathan), never over GitHub. Add per-edge echo keys so a compromised edge can't forge another edge's echos. Bake key rotation and a signed revocation frame into the protocol from day one.
wrenches.residual_risk: Foundry private-key compromise reproduces the same fleet-wide forgery; mitigate with key rotation, offline/HSM custody, and a revocation/rotation frame — but the blast radius of a foundry key leak remains total until rotation propagates.

wrenches.title: The foundation is already unauthenticated AND mutating — caller identity does not exist in the request path
wrenches.scenario: Confirmed in brainstem.py: zero inbound auth gating on ANY route. Beyond the acknowledged Leviathan POST /api/agent shell-RCE, every running brainstem already exposes POST /agents/import (drop a *_agent.py = arbitrary Python that auto-pip-installs and executes on the next request), DELETE /agents/<filename> (wipe capabilities), POST /models/set, POST /chat — all unauthenticated. 'LAN-only' is a deployment assumption, not an enforced control. One `--host 0.0.0.0`, a co-located SSRF, a malicious LAN device, a container port-map, or a tunnel (the ecosystem already uses ngrok/devtunnel for the Teams tether) converts LAN-only into internet-exposed fleet RCE. The frame-net's entire premise is reaching brainstems beyond localhost.
wrenches.impact: Single unauthenticated request = arbitrary code execution on the host, capability deletion, or model/persona hijack. At fleet scale, one exposed node bridges into every reachable brainstem. This is the load-bearing failure under every other trust claim — you cannot build planetary trust on a request path with no notion of WHO is calling.
wrenches.mitigation: Bind loopback-only by default with an explicit opt-in to expose. Require a local capability bearer token on ALL mutating routes (/chat, /agents/*, /models/*, /api/agent), generated at install, shown once, stored 0600. Per-route authZ — import/delete and shell-capable agents gated harder than read routes. Any non-loopback exposure REQUIRES token + TLS. For the frame-net, reaching an edge is THROUGH its signed twin/inbox with verify-before-act, never a raw mutating HTTP route. Add a CI invariant that fails the build if any state-mutating route lacks the auth decorator.
wrenches.residual_risk: A token leaked from a user's disk/CI still grants local control; loopback-only does not protect against a malicious local process or a co-tenant in a shared container.

wrenches.title: Identity is bound to a keypair and a repo PATH, not to a vetted edge — signature proves 'a key signed this', never 'this is the right edge'
wrenches.scenario: A public twin is a GitHub repo path (twin.json + verify key + state/inbox/outbox). Whoever creates that path first owns the identity. Attacker pre-registers `edge/mars-rover-1` (squatting), OR forks the real twin and stands up `evil/mars-rover-1` with a copied twin.json but THEIR verify key. Anyone resolving the edge by NAME (not by pinned key) talks to the impostor — and the forged twin is content-addressed and validly self-signed, so it looks legitimate. Light-delay means the real edge cannot quickly dispute. GitHub repo-jacking (renamed/deleted accounts leaving dangling references an attacker re-registers) is a known, automatable class.
wrenches.impact: Total impersonation of any node: feed peers false state, harvest echos meant for the real edge, or sit as a man-in-the-middle on its inbox. Because everything downstream trusts the twin as the edge's 'front door', a spoofed twin poisons every interaction that node has with the swarm.
wrenches.mitigation: Make identity = the public KEY fingerprint (or content hash of a signed enrollment record), NOT the path/name — the GitHub path is a mere discovery hint. Resolve and PIN edges by key; pin-by-endorsement (birth certificate) rather than pin-on-first-contact where first-use is attacker-reachable. Publish a key-transparency log so a swapped key is publicly and provably detectable. Reserve/claim namespaces via signed enrollment in the canonical registry. Monitor renamed/deleted accounts for repo-jacking.
wrenches.residual_risk: Key-as-identity shifts the whole problem onto key distribution and the transparency log's own integrity; a user who fat-fingers a fingerprint still pins the wrong edge.

wrenches.title: Frame authority capture — HMAC is symmetric, so every verifier can forge; even Ed25519 with one authority key = total swarm hijack
wrenches.scenario: Frames are swarm-shared guidance, signed (HMAC now). HMAC is symmetric: every edge that can VERIFY a frame holds the same secret that can FORGE one. In the HMAC era, ANY edge — or anyone who reads the secret from one repo, CI log, or disk image — can mint authoritative frames for the WHOLE swarm. Even with asymmetric crypto, a single frame-authority key means its compromise lets an attacker forge a poisoned echo ('treat the canyon reading as a sensor fault, proceed'; 'trust new key X'; 'wipe local state') that passes every edge's verify-before-act check because it is correctly signed.
wrenches.impact: One secret/key compromise = simultaneous control of every edge's 'aim' across the planet, delivered through the exact channel the safety model trusts. This is the single highest-leverage attack on the whole architecture.
wrenches.mitigation: NEVER ship HMAC for cross-trust-boundary frames — it conflates sign and verify. Asymmetric from day one for anything multi-party. Make frame authority a QUORUM (m-of-n threshold signature), not a single key, so one leak is not swarm capture. Keep frames as ADVICE only (the design already says guidance != commands — lean on it hard): bound any frame against local, non-overridable safety invariants so a validly-signed 'drive off the cliff' still cannot execute.
wrenches.residual_risk: Threshold signing raises operational complexity and key-custody load; a quorum compromised slowly (m custodians over time) still captures authority, just harder.

wrenches.title: Replay, rollback, and equivocation on a cached, eventually-consistent, append-only wire
wrenches.scenario: raw.githubusercontent.com is a CACHING CDN; the wire is append-only with light-delay. An attacker (or a stale cache, or a malicious mirror) can: REPLAY an old validly-signed echo ('drive forward' replayed after the edge already reached the cliff); ROLL BACK latest.json — the 'cheap heartbeat' is a trivially-spoofable pointer — to an earlier tick/hash; or EQUIVOCATE, serving edge A a different frame chain than edge B to split the swarm's worldview. Signatures alone stop none of these.
wrenches.impact: Stale-but-signed guidance drives a physical edge into a now-invalid action; rollback freezes an edge on obsolete aim; equivocation fractures the swarm into inconsistent realities that each verify cleanly. Safety-critical for any edge that moves or acts.
wrenches.mitigation: Bake monotonicity into the wire format NOW: every signed frame carries an epoch + strictly-increasing tick + timestamp; edges reject any non-increasing tick, persisting the last-seen tick across reboots (anti-rollback). Sign latest.json and gate it by the same monotonic rule. Verify prev-hash chain continuity AND publish a single signed checkpoint/transparency root so two divergent chains are detectable. Give every echo an explicit expiry ('valid until tick/time T') so replays self-expire.
wrenches.residual_risk: A determined attacker who pins an edge to a slightly-stale-but-unexpired echo within the validity window still gets a small action delta; clock/tick skew across a light-delayed fleet complicates expiry tuning.

wrenches.title: Honest operator error has no undo at light-delay, and offline-root-key LOSS (not compromise) makes the PKI unrecoverable — the whole trust model assumes malice, not mistakes
wrenches.scenario: Every crypto/identity wrench addresses an ATTACKER. None address the far more common failure: a tired, authorized human. An operator forges a perfectly-valid, correctly-signed echo with a wrong setpoint / a fat-fingered objective / a copy-paste from the wrong edge, and the system's entire job is to faithfully authenticate and deliver it. Verify-before-act verifies the signature, not the sanity — the mistake propagates fleet-wide and, at light-delay, cannot be recalled before edges act on it for a full contact window. Separately, the proposed defenses lean on an 'offline org root key' / out-of-band root. The bus-factor failure is silent: the one custodian leaves, the HSM/yubikey is lost in a move, the passphrase dies with them, the offline laptop is wiped. That is not key COMPROMISE (revocable) — it is key LOSS, which means you can never sign a new canon, a revocation list, or a kernel patch again. The PKI becomes read-only forever.
wrenches.impact: A valid, well-meant, catastrophic command with no recall mechanism at planetary delay; and a single-person-or-token loss event that bricks the ability to ever update canon, rotate keys, or push a security fix — the root of trust has no recovery story, only a compromise story.
wrenches.mitigation: Make destructive/objective-changing echos require a typed confirmation + a second human signer (threshold) + a mandatory soak/dry-run window before they can be marked current — so one human's slip can't reach the fleet. Add a 'staged, not yet active until tick N' field so there is a recall window before light-delay closes it. For root-key survivability: use M-of-N threshold custody (Shamir/multisig) across people and locations so no single loss is fatal; document and REHEARSE a key-recovery/re-anchor ceremony; pre-distribute a secondary root so loss of the primary is recoverable, not terminal. Test the recovery drill, not just the happy path.
wrenches.residual_risk: Threshold custody adds coordination latency to every legitimate canon update and can itself deadlock if too many custodians are unavailable; soak windows slow genuine emergency response; a determined-but-wrong quorum (groupthink) still ships the bad command.

wrenches.title: Immutable, append-only, content-addressed, publicly-archived telemetry is structurally illegal under GDPR/CCPA right-to-erasure — you literally cannot delete what the design forbids deleting
wrenches.scenario: The other lenses flagged public telemetry as a CONFIDENTIALITY leak. The distinct, unflagged problem is a hard REGULATORY contradiction: GDPR Art.17 and CCPA grant data subjects a right to ERASURE, and data-residency rules (GDPR, China PIPL, etc.) constrain WHERE data may live. The frame-net is deliberately append-only, content-addressed, prev-hash-chained, replicated, mirrored to GHArchive, and engineered to RESIST deletion (that immutability is sold as a feature for audit/non-repudiation). If any telemetry, message, or memory entry contains personal data (an operator's name, a location that identifies a person, anything PII-adjacent), you cannot honor an erasure request without breaking the chain — and you cannot un-publish from third-party archives at all. The system's central integrity property and a hard legal requirement are in direct opposition.
wrenches.impact: A single privacy/DPO review makes the platform non-deployable in the EU/UK/California and for any enterprise with a privacy program — not because of a leak, but because the architecture cannot DELETE by construction. 'Immutable forever, mirrored, content-addressed' is the precise opposite of 'erasable on request,' and a content-address that names a personal-data byte string cannot be revoked without invalidating everything chained after it.
wrenches.mitigation: Keep personal data OFF the immutable chain entirely: the chain stores only signed hashes/commitments; the actual payload lives in a mutable, erasable, access-controlled store (so erasure removes the payload while the hash remains a tombstone). Use crypto-shredding — encrypt per-subject payloads with per-subject keys and 'erase' by destroying the key, leaving undecryptable ciphertext on the immutable layer. Enforce data-minimization and pseudonymization at the edge before anything is committed. Document a lawful basis and a DPIA before any pilot touches personal data; never put PII in public Issues/state.json.
wrenches.residual_risk: Crypto-shredding is contested as 'erasure' by some regulators (ciphertext still exists); tombstones still leak metadata (that a record existed, its timing, its size); anything already pushed to public GitHub/GHArchive is irreversibly public regardless of forward fixes.

wrenches.title: Token-cost amplification per tick: 3-round loop × full-history resend × full agent-metadata system prompt, with no prompt caching on the Copilot path
wrenches.scenario: Each /chat reloads soul.md plus ALL agent metadata into the system prompt and runs up to 3 tool rounds (range(3)), each round resending the entire growing message history. The Copilot completions path does no prompt caching, so the static system prompt + history is re-billed every round, every tick. Cheap for one edge; at millions of edges ticking on echo cadence the token bill is super-linear in agents-loaded and conversation depth. Nobody has computed tokens-per-edge-per-day, so the CFO has no $/edge number.
wrenches.impact: The dominant scale cost is invisible and grows with agent count and history length — exactly the things users are encouraged to expand. A fleet that adds agents or holds long sessions silently multiplies spend.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank a token-budget model and caching: use provider prompt-caching (Anthropic/Azure) for the static soul+metadata prefix once off the borrowed-Copilot path, trim/lazy-load agent metadata to only relevant tools, cap and summarize history, and instrument tokens/round/edge. Make the 3-round cap and history window configurable per cost tier.
wrenches.residual_risk: Trimming tools to control tokens reduces the LLM's capability surface (it can't call a tool it wasn't shown), and prompt-caching only helps on providers that support it — the free Copilot tier likely never gets it.

wrenches.title: Synchronized contact windows create a thundering herd that trips secondary rate limits exactly when the link is most precious
wrenches.scenario: Intermittent edges reconnect in correlated bursts: orbital mechanics synchronize Mars-style contact windows, and a LAN outage recovery brings every local body back at once. They all hit GitHub simultaneously — telemetry writes, latest.json reads, /models discovery, and Copilot token exchange. With no cross-edge jitter or coordinated backoff, the burst trips the 5k/hr + 80/min secondary limits precisely during the narrow window when an edge can talk, so the contact is wasted and telemetry is dropped.
wrenches.impact: Correlated bursts convert scarce contact windows into rate-limit failures, starving the foundry of the telemetry it needs and leaving edges to keep acting on stale echos longer than designed. Cold-start /models + token-exchange storms during fleet reboots/deploys have the same shape.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank backpressure-by-design: randomized jitter on every edge's contact schedule, client token-bucket + exponential backoff with respect for Retry-After, batch all of a window's writes into one request, cache /models across the fleet (ship the model list in the echo instead of each edge discovering it), and stagger fleet deploys. Make the foundry tolerant of out-of-order/late telemetry.
wrenches.residual_risk: Jitter helps statistically but physics-synchronized windows (real orbital mechanics) can't be fully de-correlated, so the worst-case simultaneous burst still exists at the largest scale.

wrenches.title: Model-'auto' plus model-hopping on 429 is the exact traffic signature GitHub anti-abuse bans — it couples cost risk to ban risk
wrenches.scenario: On 400/429/5xx the code cycles through other available models before giving up (brainstem.py ~line 1038), and GITHUB_MODEL='auto' probes /models. At fleet scale, millions of edges hopping models to dodge rate limits is the textbook fingerprint of quota-evasion that abuse systems are tuned to detect and ban. You are training GitHub's anti-abuse to classify your traffic as adversarial, which both raises ban probability and degrades the borrowed-Copilot cost basis.
wrenches.impact: Couples the two worst risks: hitting rate limits (cost/availability) and getting account-flagged for evasion (existential transport loss). A fleet-wide ban can follow a benign rate-limit episode amplified by reflexive model-hopping.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank honest backoff: on 429 respect Retry-After and back off rather than immediately model-hopping; reserve model-fallback for genuine model-unsupported (400 unsupported_api) not for quota (429). Pin a model per cohort instead of fleet-wide 'auto' probing. Once off the borrowed-Copilot path this risk largely disappears, reinforcing the BYO-key migration.
wrenches.residual_risk: Polite backoff lowers throughput (edges wait instead of retrying elsewhere), and you remain at the mercy of GitHub's undocumented, changeable abuse heuristics regardless of how well-behaved you are.

wrenches.title: Install one-liner pulls from main — a planetary fleet bootstraps from an unpinned, unsigned, no-canary supply chain
wrenches.scenario: Per the repo model, the curl|bash one-liners pull install.sh from main, and users get whatever is on main. A planetary fleet that (re)bootstraps from 'curl ... main | bash' has zero supply-chain pinning: one bad commit, or a single compromise of the installer repo, is instantly live on every newly-provisioned or re-bootstrapping edge, with no staged rollout, no canary, no signature on the installer itself, and the installer runs as the user.
wrenches.impact: A single bad push to main is a fleet-wide outage or a fleet-wide RCE delivered through the trusted install path — the same 'main is sacred' invariant that protects you is the single chokepoint that can poison everyone at once.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank installer supply-chain hygiene: sign the installer and verify the signature before exec, pin installs to an immutable tag (BRAINSTEM_VERSION already gives rollback points — make tag-pinning the default for fleets, not main), stage rollouts via cohort tags with a canary ring before promoting, and provide a checksummed release artifact rather than live-curl of main. Protect the installer repo with required reviews + hardware-key 2FA.
wrenches.residual_risk: Pinning to tags slows security-patch propagation (the inverse risk: a known-vuln edge stays on an old pinned tag), so you trade fast-fix for blast-radius control and must manage both.

wrenches.title: Using GitHub as a CDN + app backend + telemetry datastore is an Acceptable-Use-Policy violation — one abuse report dark-kills the planetary wire atomically
wrenches.scenario: GitHub's AUP prohibits using the service as a content delivery network, for excessive automated bulk activity, and as a backend for applications/data not connected to software development. rapp-static-api/1.0 serving content over raw and Issues-as-ledger are squarely in that territory. A public, signed, append-only channel that drives shell-capable bodies also pattern-matches malware C2, which GitHub actively scans for and shares to threat-intel feeds. A single DMCA, abuse report, sanctions flag, or billing lapse on the one org removes the repo and the entire wire vanishes at once — no multi-homing, no appeal SLA.
wrenches.impact: Existential, correlated, externally-triggerable kill switch on the whole swarm that the team does not control. Worse: being labeled C2/malware infrastructure has reputational + legal blast radius beyond just the swarm going dark.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank: (1) Multi-home from day one behind the Transport interface — mirror frames to >=2 independent origins (e.g., a second git host + an object store/IPFS) so no single takedown is fatal; latest.json points into whichever is reachable. (2) Keep the GitHub footprint defensibly 'software-development shaped' (specs, signed artifacts as Releases) and move high-volume live telemetry OFF public Issues. (3) Avoid the C2 signature: never ship a swarm where a public channel can directly drive a shell agent (see Leviathan wrenches); make commands guidance-only and locally-reconciled (frame-net already does this — enforce it). (4) Engage GitHub proactively / use GitHub Apps + sanctioned APIs rather than scraping raw as a CDN.
wrenches.residual_risk: Even multi-homed, GitHub-as-primary remains a policy liability; and a determined Trust & Safety action plus the malware-infra label can still damage the project's standing even after failover.

wrenches.title: No replay/freshness enforcement: an offline-reconnecting edge can be fed an old-but-validly-signed echo and 'degrade-to-one' on stale guidance believing it is current
wrenches.scenario: Frames carry {tick} and are validly signed. An edge that has been offline (the explicit frame-net case) reconnects and reads an echo. Without per-edge monotonic-tick enforcement and an expiry, an attacker (or just CDN staleness, wrench 1) can present a whole EARLIER valid frame as 'latest'. The prev-hash chain does not prevent replay of a complete, properly-signed older frame to a node that lacks the newer ones. The edge then commits to outdated guidance and, on link loss, 'degrades-to-one' on it indefinitely — confidently wrong.
wrenches.impact: Stale/replayed guidance accepted as current, especially on exactly the intermittent edges the system is built for. An attacker needs no key — just the ability to serve/point an offline edge at an old valid frame.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank: (1) Per-edge monotonic tick: reject any echo with tick <= last-applied; persist last-applied across reboots. (2) Explicit valid_until/expiry on every echo; past expiry the edge knows its guidance is STALE and enters a conservative degraded mode rather than trusting it as current. (3) Nonce/challenge in telemetry so the foundry's next echo provably responds to the edge's latest report (freshness binding), defeating replay of pre-nonce echos. (4) Signed wall-clock + a sanity time source on the edge so 'how old is this guidance' is answerable even with no link.
wrenches.residual_risk: Edges with no trustworthy clock can't fully evaluate expiry; and a conservative degraded mode trades safety for capability — a long-offline edge may correctly refuse to act on the only (stale) guidance it has.

wrenches.title: No Transport abstraction seam — READ is hardcoded to raw and WRITE to Issues, so every failover above is a rewrite instead of a config flip
wrenches.scenario: Because the design names the concrete carriers (raw.githubusercontent.com for read, Issues API for write, Actions for the foundry) rather than an interface, there is no place to plug an alternative when any of the preceding wrenches fire. The mitigation that nearly every other wrench depends on — swap transports under ban/outage/block/scale — is architecturally absent today, so when reality hits, the team is refactoring the core under pressure instead of flipping a provider.
wrenches.impact: Converts every transport failure from a recoverable config change into an emergency redesign. This is the meta-SPOF: not GitHub itself, but the ABSENCE of a seam to leave GitHub.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank and do early: (1) Define a Transport interface NOW — read_object(hash), read_pointer(), append(record), even while GitHub is the only implementation; route ALL carrier access through it. (2) Keep frames/echos/telemetry as transport-agnostic signed, content-addressed objects (they already are) so any carrier just moves opaque blobs. (3) Stand up a SECOND trivial implementation (object store or a second git host) purely to prove the seam is real and not GitHub-shaped. (4) Add a transport-health + failover policy so degrade/switch is automatic.
wrenches.residual_risk: An interface defined too closely around GitHub's semantics (mutable issues, CDN reads) leaks those assumptions into callers; a truly carrier-neutral abstraction (esp. for store-and-forward/DTN) needs deliberate design, not just a wrapper.

wrenches.title: Kernel-lock blocks security remediation of the kernel's own attack surface
wrenches.scenario: The kernel is frozen by a CI invariant and 'every capability ships as an agent, never an engine edit.' But the worst vulnerabilities live IN the kernel: the 0.0.0.0 bind, wildcard CORS, unauthenticated /chat and /agents/import, and _auto_install. You cannot patch those as a drop-in agent. The governance principle that makes the platform teachable directly obstructs shipping a security fix.
wrenches.impact: When a CVE lands on the kernel surface, the response process is fighting its own constitution. Slow or blocked patching of a remotely-reachable RCE is itself a finding a reviewer escalates.
wrenches.mitigation: Carve out an explicit, fast-track security-exception path in the governance model: kernel security patches are permitted, expedited, and signed; define a security SLA and a versioned-rollback story (you already tag brainstem-vX.Y.Z) for emergency releases. Pull the security-sensitive surface (bind/CORS/auth) into a small, hardened, separately-reviewed kernel core that CAN change for security without violating the capability-as-agent rule.
wrenches.residual_risk: Each kernel exception erodes the lock's guarantee and the 'engine never changes' teaching story; emergency releases pressure the one-liner-install 'main is always working' promise.

wrenches.title: Memory / ContextMemory is an unsigned, auto-injected, self-reinforcing prompt-injection reservoir
wrenches.scenario: /chat auto-injects <memory> into the system prompt when ContextMemory is loaded. A single successful injection (one poisoned echo, one crafted /chat, one inbox message) that writes to memory achieves PERSISTENCE: every future turn re-injects the poisoned memory, surviving restarts, never signed, never re-verified. On a frame-net edge it compounds — the 'keep thinking on last echo' edge re-poisons itself from its own memory every tick, forever, with no external attacker needed after the first write.
wrenches.impact: One-shot injection becomes permanent, self-healing compromise of the agent's behavior and the edge's autonomous loop, invisible to signature-based controls because memory was never in the trust model.
wrenches.mitigation: Treat memory as untrusted data with provenance: tag every memory entry with who/what wrote it and never let memory content be interpreted as instructions; require explicit, rate-limited, attributable writes (no silent self-write from interpreted guidance); provide a 'memory diff/quarantine' surface and a hard reset; on edges, expire or re-derive memory from verified state rather than free-running it.
wrenches.residual_risk: Provenance tagging doesn't stop a determined injection from writing plausible-looking 'facts'; aggressive memory expiry undercuts the feature's value.

wrenches.title: Signed .egg teaches users 'signed = safe' — pre-arming the social-engineering exploit for the first leaked key
wrenches.scenario: A .egg is a signed, portable, self-bootstrapping wrapped-organism (per project notes the brainstem itself ports as an egg that runs the install one-liner). The signature proves origin, NOT safety: a signed egg can carry exfiltrating agents, embedded/committed secrets, or agents that phone home. Worse, the UX is training users that the signature lock-icon means 'trust and run' — so the day ANY signing key leaks (see HMAC/foundry/maintainer wrenches), a signed malicious egg → user trusts it → install one-liner → RCE, and the user's mental model says it was safe.
wrenches.impact: The trust UX becomes the attack's delivery mechanism. A single key compromise converts into mass RCE because users were taught the signature is a safety verdict, not an identity claim.
wrenches.mitigation: In UX and docs, signature == 'who made this,' never 'this is safe' — show identity + reputation, require a separate consent for code that bootstraps/installs; sandbox/dry-run an egg's agents before granting filesystem/network/shell; static-scan eggs for embedded secrets and outbound calls at import; capability-manifest an egg must declare (and the user approves) what it touches; fast key-revocation + 'untrust everything signed by this key since T' kill-switch.
wrenches.residual_risk: Users click through consent; static scanning misses obfuscated payloads; sandboxing a self-bootstrapping organism that legitimately needs install rights is a contradiction.

wrenches.title: Replay / rollback of a validly-signed OLD frame — signatures never expire, and 'old' is normal on this net
wrenches.scenario: Distinct from pointer-spoofing: even if the edge fetches frames directly, a signature minted 3 ticks ago is cryptographically identical-valid today. On a seconds-to-days light-delay net the edge cannot easily distinguish a legitimately-delayed frame from a maliciously-captured-and-replayed one. Attacker re-injects a past 'abort' or 'safe-mode' echo at a moment when it's harmful. The prev_hash chain doesn't save you if the edge accepts any signed frame rather than only frames that extend its KNOWN head.
wrenches.impact: Behavioral rollback / stale-command injection without breaking crypto; can repeatedly stall or misdirect an edge.
wrenches.mitigation: Make freshness intrinsic to the frame, not just the pointer: include a monotonic per-edge tick and a signed valid-for-N-ticks / not-after, and require each accepted frame to extend the edge's persisted chain head (reject frames that don't continue the known head). Highest-tick-wins, never go backward, persisted across reboots. Echos bound to a tick range so a stale echo self-expires.
wrenches.residual_risk: Wide acceptance windows for long-offline edges (a feature of degrade-to-one) re-open the replay surface near the window edge; tick-floor relies on durable storage that can be wiped.

wrenches.title: Runaway autonomous loop → actuator/battery burn and reconnect telemetry storm
wrenches.scenario: The /chat tool-loop is bounded to 3 rounds per request, but nothing bounds re-invocation across requests, scheduled agents, or an echo that prescribes a recurring behavior. Offline with no human, an edge enters a self-reinforcing loop: act → telemetry send fails → retry/re-act → burn battery and actuator cycles → on reconnect, flush a flood of queued Issue writes (a self-DoS / telemetry storm against the GitHub Issues API).
wrenches.impact: Battery and actuator depletion, GitHub secondary-rate-limit / abuse-flag on reconnect, frame foundry overwhelmed by a backlog spike, possible account suspension.
wrenches.mitigation: Hard local governors INDEPENDENT of cognition: max-actions-per-interval, energy budget, actuator duty-cycle caps, and a watchdog that forces safe-state on loop detection. Telemetry must coalesce/batch with backpressure on reconnect (one summarizing append, not N raw events) and jittered backoff. Idempotency keys so a looped/replayed action does not double-execute.
wrenches.residual_risk: Governors can throttle a legitimately urgent burst; slow loops evade detection heuristics; coalescing can drop forensically-important detail.

wrenches.title: Revocation latency equals connectivity latency — you can't recall a key from a disconnected edge
wrenches.scenario: You discover an edge or forge key is compromised and publish a revocation. But the target edge is mid-blackout for days. It keeps acting on the now-revoked key/echo for the entire window. Revocation only takes effect when the edge can reach the network — the dark twin of 'degrade-to-one.'
wrenches.impact: A known-bad key or echo keeps driving an edge for days after operators believe they 'fixed' it; no software remote-kill exists for an off-grid edge.
wrenches.mitigation: Make safety fail-safe by TTL, not by reachability: short echo validity windows so a stale (possibly-revoked) echo expires on its own. Require periodic positive reaffirmation ('heartbeat-to-act'): no fresh signed latest.json within N → autonomy downgrades automatically. Carry the current revocation epoch INSIDE every frame so the chain self-distributes revocation; require the freshest epoch for destructive actions.
wrenches.residual_risk: A truly offline edge already executing a destructive action cannot be stopped; fundamental tension between autonomy duration and recall safety; fail-safe-off can strand a needed function.

wrenches.title: latest.json heartbeat is a forgeable/replayable liveness lie, and raw CDN caching makes it silently stale
wrenches.scenario: latest.json {tick,hash,updated} is the cheap heartbeat pointer. An attacker (or simply a stale CDN edge) can serve an OLD latest.json so an edge believes its guidance is current when it isn't, defeating every TTL/freshness control above. raw.githubusercontent.com is CDN-cached with its own lag, so an edge can silently read a latest.json minutes-to-hours behind the true head. 'Verify the hash' verifies integrity, never recency.
wrenches.impact: An edge believes it is fresh while acting on stale guidance — neutralizing the entire freshness-budget safety architecture; liveness spoofing; CDN-induced silent staleness.
wrenches.mitigation: latest.json carries a SIGNED monotonic tick + foundry timestamp; the edge enforces monotonicity (never accept a tick ≤ last-seen) → replays rejected. Measure freshness by signed counter + edge clock, with cache-busting commit-pinned raw reads (not branch-head) and an explicit max-staleness tolerance that ASSUMES the CDN is N minutes behind. Never trust the unsigned 'updated' field.
wrenches.residual_risk: Edge clock skew/spoofing (see W15); monotonic counter resets on forge-key rotation; CDN lag bounds the best achievable freshness.

wrenches.title: Copilot ToS and per-account rate limits make fleet cognition a thundering-herd outage on reconnect
wrenches.scenario: On contact, many edges hit Copilot through few (possibly shared) GitHub accounts. Copilot is rate-limited and ToS-scoped to interactive coding, not headless fleet inference. A reconnect storm = a thundering herd against one rate-limit bucket; one account = one bucket = a swarm-wide cognition outage exactly when edges most need to think (mirrors the WorkIQ incident where one shared M365 Copilot limit throttled the account 30+ minutes). Plus ToS-violation suspension risk.
wrenches.impact: Cognition outage at the worst moment (reconnect), account suspension for headless/fleet abuse, and legal exposure on terms of use.
wrenches.mitigation: Per-edge identity and quota via Entra Agent ID + a sanctioned fleet inference endpoint (Azure OpenAI), NOT shared personal Copilot — this is where Agent 365 genuinely helps. Stagger reconnect cognition with jittered backoff to avoid the herd. Get explicit ToS clearance for headless/fleet inference. Local SLM (W1) removes the dependency for routine cognition entirely.
wrenches.residual_risk: Per-edge cloud inference cost at planetary scale; Azure OpenAI quota is still finite; ToS clarity must be obtained in writing.

wrenches.title: Replay and reordering attacks on the append-only telemetry/echo log
wrenches.scenario: Append-only signed logs are vulnerable to REPLAY (re-submitting an old, still-validly-signed telemetry/echo to re-trigger a past action or rewind perceived state) and REORDERING (intermittent links deliver out of order, so the foundry forges on a misordered view). A valid old signature stays valid forever — signing alone does not stop replay.
wrenches.impact: An edge re-executes a past command; the foundry reconstructs a wrong world; perceived-state rollback drives bad echos.
wrenches.mitigation: Every signed message includes a monotonic nonce/tick + edge-id; each consumer tracks a per-edge high-water mark and rejects anything ≤ seen (anti-replay). The foundry orders by signed tick, not arrival time. Actions are idempotent and carry a unique action-id for execute-once semantics. Bound the acceptance window.
wrenches.residual_risk: High-water-mark state must survive edge reboot (persist it); tick rollover; legitimate retransmits must be distinguishable from replays (idempotency handles this but adds bookkeeping).

wrenches.title: The universal 'safe-state / hold' fallback is itself catastrophic in inaction-fatal domains
wrenches.scenario: Nearly every mitigation above leans on 'degrade to conservative / safe-hold.' But for many real edges DOING NOTHING is the dangerous act: a rover on a slope, a process that must keep a reactor cool, a comms relay siblings depend on. There is no per-domain definition of 'safe,' and an LLM asked to pick a safe action under uncertainty may pick the wrong one.
wrenches.impact: The universal fallback causes harm where inaction is fatal; over-conservatism elsewhere makes the system useless or unsafe — the failsafe becomes the hazard.
wrenches.mitigation: Require each edge/domain to declare a DOMAIN-SPECIFIC FAILSAFE ROUTINE — deterministic, validated, NO-LLM (e.g., 'maintain current setpoint,' 'return to base,' 'keep relaying'). This routine is part of the signed deployment and is exactly what 'degrade-to-one' executes; the LLM only enhances above this floor. Validate the failsafe per domain like safety-critical firmware, not as an LLM choice.
wrenches.residual_risk: Authoring/validating per-domain failsafes is heavy; a failsafe can still be wrong if reality falls outside its design assumptions; composing multiple edges' failsafes can conflict at the swarm level.

wrenches.title: latest.json pointer/content TOCTOU across the raw CDN
wrenches.scenario: The frame chain is immutable/content-addressed, but latest.json is a single MUTABLE pointer served separately over raw.githubusercontent.com, which is a CDN with its own ~5-minute, per-PoP cache. An edge reads latest.json -> {tick:N, hash:H}, then fetches frame H and gets a 404 because the frame blob hasn't propagated to that PoP yet, or it reads a stale latest.json pointing at H-1 while the foundry has already advanced. The pointer and the content it names are never atomically consistent, and there is no read-your-writes guarantee across two CDN objects.
wrenches.impact: Edges stall on a dangling pointer, or silently act on guidance one or more generations stale while believing it current. The 'cheap heartbeat' is a lie under load: it can point into the future (not-yet-propagated) or the past (stale cache).
wrenches.mitigation: Treat latest.json strictly as a HINT, never as truth. Verify by (a) fetching the named frame, (b) checking its signature, (c) confirming it chains via prev-hash from the last head the edge durably trusts, and (d) confirming its embedded tick is strictly > last-applied. On 404, retry with jittered backoff and a cache-busting query param; tolerate a bounded propagation window. Sign latest.json itself and embed the tick so a stale/forged pointer is detectable. Frames must self-describe their tick so the pointer is never load-bearing for correctness.
wrenches.residual_risk: A determined attacker or a long CDN cache can still pin an edge to a stale-but-valid head within the staleness window; you can detect 'not advancing' but cannot force-fresh a globally cached object.

wrenches.title: Clock skew on tick/sol — wall-clock causality on a body where wall-clock is meaningless
wrenches.scenario: The literal use case is 'a brainstem on Mars' with seconds-to-days light delay and Mars sol != Earth UTC. If 'tick' or telemetry ordering is derived from any wall-clock, an edge whose RTC drifts, resets on power loss, or runs on sol-time emits telemetry stamped in the past or far future. The foundry then reorders by timestamp, discards 'too-old' valid telemetry, or trusts stale data as freshest. A reset RTC (tick=0) makes a stale edge look brand-new or vice versa.
wrenches.impact: Causality inversions: echos forged from stale telemetry believed current; fresh observations dropped as 'expired'; ordering across edges becomes attacker-influenceable by lying about time.
wrenches.mitigation: Never use wall-clock for causality. Tick = a foundry-assigned monotonic SEQUENCE on the frame chain (logical clock). Edge telemetry carries a strictly-monotone per-edge sequence (edge_seq) and treats edge_local_time as advisory metadata only. Foundry orders/dedupes by (edge_id, edge_seq), not timestamps. Consider Lamport/vector clocks for cross-edge causality. Explicitly document and bound the maximum tolerated skew; flag edges whose advisory time is wildly inconsistent for human review rather than silently trusting or dropping.
wrenches.residual_risk: Per-edge monotonicity survives clock chaos, but a hard RTC+state wipe on an edge (true cold reboot losing edge_seq) still risks a sequence restart that looks like replay; needs durable seq persistence and a re-enrollment handshake.

wrenches.title: Out-of-order, lost, and duplicate telemetry over an intermittent link — record idempotency undefined
wrenches.scenario: Over a link with seconds-to-days delay and frequent drops, telemetry arrives out of order, in batches, or duplicated: an edge retries after a perceived send failure that actually succeeded, posting the same observation twice. If telemetry records are deltas/events rather than idempotent snapshots, the foundry double-counts ('consumed 2L fuel' applied twice).
wrenches.impact: Foundry world-model corruption from double-application or gaps -> systematically wrong echos that look authoritative.
wrenches.mitigation: Every telemetry record carries a stable idempotency key (edge_id + edge_seq + content-hash); foundry dedupes on it. Prefer last-writer-wins STATE SNAPSHOTS per edge (naturally idempotent) over event deltas. Where events are required, carry seq numbers so the foundry can detect gaps and request resend via the edge's inbox. Foundry processing must be a pure function of the deduped, ordered record SET, not of arrival order.
wrenches.residual_risk: Snapshot semantics lose intermediate transitions (a thruster fired and recovered between two snapshots is invisible); event-mode regains them but reintroduces gap/replay complexity.

wrenches.title: Twin state.json vs live brainstem: stale last-known masquerades as current state
wrenches.scenario: Each edge has a public twin with state.json 'last-known', and OTHERS reach the edge through its twin, not its live link. During a long blackout the twin's state.json is stale, but a peer planning a joint action reads it as the edge's state. The link returns and live reality contradicts the twin. The design doesn't define who wins (twin vs live) or how peers weigh staleness.
wrenches.impact: Cross-edge coordination against phantom state — peers commit to plans premised on where an edge WAS, not where it IS, then collide with reality on reconnect.
wrenches.mitigation: state.json must carry its own as-of tick and an explicit staleness/age. Consumers MUST decay confidence with age and treat twin state as last-known-with-age, never as current. On contact, the live brainstem reconciles and the twin is updated atomically (single signed write). Define the precedence rule explicitly: live, when reachable, supersedes twin; twin is advisory between contacts. Safety-critical joint actions require a fresh handshake, not a twin read.
wrenches.residual_risk: Some coordination genuinely cannot wait for a round-trip at days of light-delay, so peers must act on aged state by necessity; confidence-decay reduces but cannot eliminate the phantom-state risk.

wrenches.title: Replay / rollback of validly-signed old frames and echos
wrenches.scenario: Frames/echos are content-addressed and signed, but an OLD frame/echo is still validly signed forever. A stale CDN, a malicious relay, or an attacker re-serves frame N-5 (or echo N-3) as if current. Because DEGRADE-TO-ONE means edges trust their last echo, feeding an edge an old-but-valid echo silently reverts its aim to past guidance — a time-travel attack the design's own resilience model amplifies.
wrenches.impact: An attacker rewinds individual edges or the swarm to superseded guidance using only legitimately-signed artifacts; no signature check catches it because the signature is genuine.
wrenches.mitigation: Consumers enforce strict MONOTONICITY: never accept a frame/echo whose tick/seq is <= last-applied, and persist last-applied durably across reboots. Sign latest.json and make it monotone. Distinguish 'no new guidance' from 'being replayed' with a staleness alarm when the tick fails to advance beyond an expected cadence. Echos carry a per-edge monotone echo-seq so an old echo for that edge is rejected.
wrenches.residual_risk: Within the legitimate staleness window (slow link, days between echos) an edge cannot tell 'genuinely old because no new guidance' from 'maliciously replayed old'; monotonicity blocks rollback but not being pinned at the current head.

wrenches.title: New-edge trust bootstrap: TOFU over the untrusted GitHub wire owns an edge from birth
wrenches.scenario: A new edge must learn the foundry's verify key and the genesis hash to start verifying anything. If it fetches those over the same untrusted GitHub/raw wire at first boot, a MITM, a typosquatted repo, or a poisoned .egg substitutes an attacker key. The edge then trusts attacker-forged frames/echos forever — trust-on-first-use against an adversary who controls first use.
wrenches.impact: Edge compromised from birth with no later signature failure to reveal it — every malicious frame verifies against the attacker key the edge was tricked into pinning.
wrenches.mitigation: Bake the foundry public verify key + genesis hash into the .egg / edge image OUT-OF-BAND via signed provisioning — never fetch the root of trust over the wire it secures. Pin the key (certificate-pinning style). Sign .eggs and verify the signature before an edge accepts its embedded trust roots. Treat enrollment as a deliberate, audited step, not an automatic first-contact fetch.
wrenches.residual_risk: Supply-chain compromise of the .egg build/signing pipeline reproduces the problem upstream; key rotation requires a trusted channel to every already-deployed edge, which a deep-space edge may lack.

wrenches.title: Action idempotency: re-reading an inbox echo after reboot re-executes the physical action
wrenches.scenario: An echo says 'do X.' The link flaps or the edge reboots, it re-reads its inbox/, re-encounters the same still-present echo, and re-executes X. If X is a real-world action (fire thruster, deploy, transmit) rather than a pure state update, you get a double-action. Inbox-as-mailbox means consumed echos persist and look freshly-actionable after any state loss.
wrenches.impact: Physical double-execution of irreversible actions — the most expensive failure class, triggered by ordinary reboots and link flaps, not even an attack.
wrenches.mitigation: Edge durably persists 'last-applied echo-seq' and NEVER re-executes an echo at or below it, even after reboot. Echos carry an idempotency token. Design edge actions to be idempotent or guard them behind a local state machine that records 'already did X for echo N'. Inbox items are untrusted, replayable transport — application is gated by durable local state, not by presence in the inbox.
wrenches.residual_risk: If the durable last-applied store is itself lost in the same fault that triggers the reboot, the guard fails; needs the seq store on the same durability tier as the action's own commit (or a hardware-level interlock).

wrenches.title: Force-push / history rewrite quietly violates 'append-only' on a mutable git branch
wrenches.scenario: The frame chain lives on a git branch that, absent protection, permits force-push and history rewrite. A compromised or careless writer force-pushes, rewriting frames that consumers already cached. The prev-hash chain is internally consistent on the new history, but edges that pinned the old head now descend from a history that no longer exists — append-only is broken by the underlying VCS, not the protocol.
wrenches.impact: Silent divergence between edges that cached pre-rewrite frames and the new canonical history; the immutability the whole consistency model assumes is unenforced at the storage layer.
wrenches.mitigation: Protected branch with force-push DISABLED, required linear history, and no direct human pushes (foundry-only via reviewed workflow). Consumers durably pin their last-trusted head and ALARM on any head that doesn't descend from it (a rewrite is then detectable, not silent). Periodic external attestation (e.g., publish head hashes to a second immutable store) so a rewrite is caught even by edges that were offline during it.
wrenches.residual_risk: An offline edge that reconnects after a rewrite sees only the new history and may accept it as canonical if its pinned head was pruned; external attestation helps only for edges that can reach the second store.

wrenches.title: Telemetry poisoning — lie to the foundry through the channel it trusts and it forges an honest-but-poisoned echo
wrenches.scenario: Edges WRITE telemetry append-only via the Issues API; the foundry forges echos FROM that telemetry. If telemetry submission is unauthenticated (public-repo Issues are often world-writable) or an edge token is stolen, an attacker injects fake telemetry — spoof another edge's position/health, or flood garbage. The foundry, trusting its input, dutifully forges a poisoned echo. This bypasses authority capture and foundry compromise entirely: you don't break the crypto, you feed it garbage. Issues comments are trivially scriptable.
wrenches.impact: GIGO at planetary scale: misdirect edges, mask a failing node, or DoS the foundry with junk it must process. The forged echo is fully legitimate end-to-end, so no signature check catches it.
wrenches.mitigation: Every telemetry item must be SIGNED by the originating edge's key; the foundry verifies origin before ingesting — no anonymous Issue becomes an echo input. Gate submission (dedicated app token / authenticated path), not open Issues. Foundry applies per-edge rate caps and plausibility/anomaly filtering; conflicting or implausible telemetry gets flagged, not forged. Provide a telemetry-dispute path so an edge can repudiate spoofed entries attributed to it.
wrenches.residual_risk: An edge whose own key is compromised can submit signed-but-false telemetry; plausibility filters have false negatives and can be gamed by slow, in-range poisoning.

wrenches.title: No revocation story over a one-way, light-delayed, append-only, server-less wire
wrenches.scenario: A key leaks — now what? There is no CRL/OCSP; transport is a caching CDN (read) plus append-only Issues (write). (a) CDN caching makes revocation propagate slowly and lets an attacker keep serving a stale 'still valid' view. (b) Append-only means you cannot un-publish the frames the attacker signed during the compromise window. (c) A degrade-to-one edge on Mars may not learn of the revocation for DAYS and keeps acting on echos signed by the leaked key. (d) If the attacker revokes/rotates FIRST, they lock out the legitimate holder — revocation becomes a DoS.
wrenches.impact: Key compromise has an unbounded, unrecallable blast radius; the very mechanism meant to contain it can be weaponized to deny service. For a fleet of physical edges this is potentially irreversible (an edge acts on revoked guidance before it ever hears 'stop trusting that key').
wrenches.mitigation: Design revocation IN NOW, not later. Short-lived signing keys with frequent rotation so the default blast radius is a window, not forever. A signed, monotonic key-transparency/revocation log that is itself part of the verify path. Embed key validity windows + epoch in every frame so an edge rejects anything signed outside the current epoch. Authenticated, anti-rollback revocation (only the holder or quorum can revoke). Define explicit behavior for an offline edge that later discovers it acted on a since-revoked echo: quarantine + re-verify.
wrenches.residual_risk: Short key lifetimes increase rotation operational risk and re-enrollment load for genuinely-offline edges; the revocation log itself becomes a high-value target and availability dependency.

wrenches.title: HMAC-now creates a downgrade attack and an unattributable shared-secret leak during crypto migration
wrenches.scenario: The plan is HMAC now -> Ed25519 -> ECDSA later. During coexistence an attacker forces a DOWNGRADE: present an HMAC-signed frame to an edge still accepting HMAC for back-compat even after asymmetric rolls out, pinning everyone to the weakest accepted suite. Worse, bootstrapping HMAC means distributing ONE shared secret to N edges — the secret is burned the moment ANY of the N is imaged/leaked, and with a shared secret you cannot tell WHICH edge leaked it (no attribution). The accepted-suite config is itself carried/negotiated in unauthenticated frames.
wrenches.impact: Silent reduction of the whole swarm's signing strength to its weakest tolerated algorithm, plus an un-attributable, swarm-wide secret exposure that you cannot scope or trace to a culprit.
wrenches.mitigation: Treat HMAC as DEV-ONLY / single-trust-domain — never let HMAC and asymmetric be acceptable for the same trust boundary; no negotiated downgrade, pin the minimum suite per epoch from the authority side. Use PER-EDGE keys from day one even in the HMAC phase so leaks are attributable and scoped. Bake a hard 'minimum acceptable sig_suite' floor into the edge safety kernel that no frame can lower.
wrenches.residual_risk: Pinning minimum suites complicates legitimate gradual rollout; a hard floor can strand older edges that cannot upgrade their crypto.

wrenches.title: Confused-deputy: the foundry/twin holds the union of all authority and the inbox is an unauthenticated command channel in mailbox clothing
wrenches.scenario: The foundry reads ALL edges' telemetry and writes ALL edges' echos — a deputy holding union-of-all authority. Subtle attacks: get it to apply edge A's telemetry to edge B's echo (cross-wiring), or let a low-trust edge's telemetry influence a high-trust edge's guidance (privilege escalation via aggregation) — any cross-edge inference path is an authority-laundering path. Separately, the public twin 'animates on contact' from inbox/: if anyone can drop an 'echo' or 'message' into inbox/, that is an unauthenticated command channel wearing a mailbox costume, and an edge that trusts position-in-mailbox over signature is owned.
wrenches.impact: Authority laundering and privilege escalation through aggregation, plus a direct injection path if inbox writes aren't authenticated. Turns the 'reach the edge through its twin' convenience into an attack surface.
wrenches.mitigation: Strict origin-binding and least authority in the foundry: edge B's echo is a pure function of B's OWN signed telemetry plus the swarm frame, with explicit, audited, signed rules for any cross-edge influence. Capability-scope foundry outputs — an echo carries only typed guidance within a declared schema, never arbitrary instructions/code. Inbox writes must be authenticated; the edge verifies sender + signature before treating anything as an echo, never trusting mailbox position. Separate 'message from a peer' (low-trust, advisory) from 'echo from the foundry' (quorum/authority-signed) at the verify layer.
wrenches.residual_risk: Some legitimate cross-edge coordination is genuinely useful and hard to fully constrain by schema; a signed-but-adversarial peer message still reaches the edge as advisory input.

wrenches.title: Frozen kernel vs churning upstream: Copilot auth/model deprecation, GitHub API changes, and spec-version proliferation rot the kernel from the outside — drift the lock cannot stop
wrenches.scenario: The lock freezes the kernel's BYTES, but the kernel depends on moving external surfaces it does not control: the GitHub Copilot device-code OAuth flow and token-exchange endpoints, specific model ids (the default 'auto' picks 'the highest Claude Sonnet the account can use'), the GitHub Issues/contents/raw API shapes, and the pip/PyPI install path. All of those churn continuously — Microsoft deprecates a model id, changes the token-exchange response, tightens an API, retires an OAuth scope — and a FROZEN kernel cannot adapt by definition. Meanwhile the spec ecosystem proliferates versions (rapp-frame/1.0, rapp-moment/1.0, rapp-static-api/1.0, rappid forms...) and old canonical twins for superseded specs go stale while edges still reference them. This is 'ecosystem drift despite the lock': the lock holds the kernel still while the ground moves, guaranteeing a future day the byte-identical-to-grail kernel simply stops authenticating or stops parsing a response.
wrenches.impact: Silent, scheduled-by-someone-else death of Tier 1: the day GitHub deprecates the model or changes the auth response, every frozen edge loses its brain or its login at once, and the no-engine-edit rule forbids the adaptation. Spec-version sprawl means edges and twins disagree about which canonical hash is authoritative for which spec version, reintroducing drift the content-addressing was supposed to eliminate. The lock converts normal upstream churn into correlated, unpatchable outages.
