wrenches.mitigation: Isolate all volatile upstream contracts behind a thin, updatable ADAPTER layer that is explicitly NOT part of the frozen kernel (auth flow, model selection, API shapes live in a cartridge/adapter that can change without touching grail) — so upstream churn is absorbed at the edge of the system, not in the locked core. Pin and capability-detect model availability with graceful fallback; monitor GitHub/Copilot deprecation notices as a release-blocking signal. Define spec-version lifecycle and migration (deprecation windows, which canonical twin is current per version) as first-class, with rapp-god alarming on stale-but-referenced canon. Treat 'upstream changed' as an expected event with an owned response path.
wrenches.residual_risk: An adapter layer that absorbs upstream change is itself mutable code that must be secured and signed (re-creating supply-chain surface); some upstream changes are non-adaptable (a removed capability has no fallback); spec lifecycle governance adds process overhead the 'engine, not experience' ethos resists.

wrenches.title: raw.githubusercontent.com is a ~5-minute-TTL CDN with no SLA and geo-blocking — the 'heartbeat' lies and the transport is non-uniform
wrenches.scenario: latest.json {tick,hash,updated} and all frames/echos are READ via raw.githubusercontent.com, which is a Fastly CDN that caches content for minutes with eventual purge. So a freshly published frame is not globally visible immediately, two edges can read different 'latest' simultaneously, and the cheap heartbeat pointer can be minutes stale. raw also has no uptime SLA, is rate-limited per-IP, and is blocked or degraded in some networks and countries. For Mars-delay this staleness is fine; for the LAN/synchronous Leviathan path it's a silent correctness bug.
wrenches.impact: Heartbeat staleness causes split-brain (edges acting on different 'latest'), masks foundry outages (edges think the spine is alive because the cached pointer hasn't expired), and makes parts of the fleet simply unreachable in blocked regions — non-uniform availability you can't observe.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank transport-realism: treat latest.json as eventually-consistent with an explicit max-staleness budget surfaced to the edge LLM ('this echo may be up to N min old'), use cache-busting / the API (not raw) where freshness matters, add a monotonic tick + signed timestamp so an edge can detect a frozen pointer, and provide alternate mirrors/CDN for geo-blocked regions. Never use raw for the synchronous LAN path.
wrenches.residual_risk: Cache-busting via the API reintroduces the per-account rate-limit problem, and you can't fully fix geo-blocking without infrastructure you don't control.

wrenches.title: Frame/echo poisoning: 'verify-before-act' checks the signature, not the wisdom — prompt-injection via the guidance channel
wrenches.scenario: Frames are guidance the edge LLM reconciles against local reality and decides on. Verify confirms the signature is valid, not that the guidance is benign. An insider, a compromised signer (see HMAC wrench), or a crafted-but-valid echo can inject subtly wrong guidance ('reduce verification threshold', 'trust source X', 'the canonical hash is now Y') that passes verification and is fed straight into the edge's LLM context — classic prompt-injection, at planetary scale, through the very channel designed to steer behavior. Memory note: LLM-judge/guidance is uncalibrated, so the edge can't reliably tell good guidance from bad.
wrenches.impact: Valid-signature poisoned guidance bypasses 'verify-before-act' and steers reconciling LLMs fleet-wide toward attacker goals, including ones that weaken later verification — a self-amplifying compromise.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank semantic guardrails: constrain what guidance CAN change (echos carry data/aims, never security policy, trust roots, or executable instructions — those require a separate, more-tightly-signed channel), have the edge treat guidance as untrusted input with injection-hardened prompting, require multi-signer quorum for high-impact frames, and rate-limit how fast guidance can move an edge's behavior (no single echo can swing it past a bound).
wrenches.residual_risk: An edge LLM reconciling 'guidance vs local reality' is inherently a judgment call that can be manipulated within the allowed envelope, and quorum/guardrails add latency and complexity that slow legitimate fast-response scenarios.

wrenches.title: Observability across a planet with no server: you're blind by design, and the only way to see the fleet is itself a quota/compute cost
wrenches.scenario: There is no server, so 'how is the fleet doing' means aggregating millions of outbox/ telemetry files — which requires yet another GitHub Action scanning at scale (more Actions minutes, more API quota). There is no real-time alerting: a Mars edge hallucinating for 3 light-days won't surface until its next contact window, and a LAN body that's wedged is invisible until it next writes. SRE gets no dashboard, no SLO, no paging — the architecture removes the control plane on purpose.
wrenches.impact: Undetectable partial failures: cohorts can be silently dark, looping, or poisoned and you learn only at the next contact (possibly days later). You cannot run an SLO or an incident response against a fleet you cannot see in near-real-time.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank an observability tier: lightweight per-edge health beacons (a tiny signed status.json the twin updates) cheaper than full telemetry, a roll-up Action that emits fleet SLO metrics on a budget, dead-man's-switch alerting (alert when a twin's status goes stale past its expected contact window), and accept-staleness dashboards that show 'last-known + age' rather than pretending real-time. Define SLOs in terms of contact-window freshness, not seconds.
wrenches.residual_risk: Real-time visibility is fundamentally impossible for light-delayed edges; the best you achieve is bounded-staleness awareness, so some failures are only ever knowable after the fact.

wrenches.title: GitHub is hard-blocked for whole classes of edges (GFW, sanctions, corp proxies, air-gap) — and a real Mars rover cannot reach github.com at all
wrenches.scenario: The transport assumes Earth-internet reachability to github.com + raw + api. China's GFW intermittently blocks/raw-throttles GitHub; Iran/Russia/sanctioned regions face geofencing or legal blocks; many enterprise/gov networks proxy-strip or denylist raw.githubusercontent.com; air-gapped facilities have no path. The flagship 'brainstem on Mars' is aspirational fiction at the transport layer: there is no IP route to github.com from Mars — comms are scheduled DSN passes via relay orbiters (DTN/Bundle Protocol), not HTTP polling. For an entire class of edges, this is not degraded transport, it is zero transport.
wrenches.impact: Dark edges by geography/policy with no warning at design time. The most marketable use case (extreme-edge, intermittent, light-delay) is the one the chosen transport categorically cannot serve. Undermines the planetary-frame thesis at its premise.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank: (1) Reframe GitHub as the DEFAULT Earth-connected transport, not THE wire; the Transport interface must admit store-and-forward bundle transports (DTN/RFC 9171 Bundle Protocol) for true edge. (2) Add a relay/gateway concept: an Earth-side twin that speaks GitHub on one side and bundles/DSN/sneakernet on the other, so the off-net edge syncs frames/echos as opaque signed bundles whenever a pass opens. (3) Make frames/echos transport-agnostic signed objects (already content-addressed) so they survive any carrier — email, USB, satellite, S3. (4) Document explicitly that synchronous reachability is a transport property, not a swarm property.
wrenches.residual_risk: You can carry signed objects over any medium, but the cadence/latency of a blocked or interplanetary edge is bounded by the carrier (hours-to-days), so 'planetary frame-net' must promise eventual, not timely, consistency for those edges — a real scope cut from the pitch.

wrenches.title: raw.githubusercontent.com refuses to serve files >100MB — the append-only ledger eventually exceeds the serving limit and the heartbeat dies
wrenches.scenario: Frames are append-only and chained; state.json/the frame log grows monotonically. raw will not serve any file over 100MB (hard limit), GitHub blocks pushes of single files >100MB, and repos accumulate Git object bloat that degrades clone/fetch and trips ~1-5GB repo-size warnings. The day an append pushes the served log past 100MB, raw returns an error and the heartbeat/state read silently breaks for every reader at once.
wrenches.impact: A guaranteed future cliff, not a probabilistic one: append-only + a hard serving ceiling = certain eventual failure of the read path. Likely hits first at the busiest (most valuable) edges/repos. Repo bloat also slows every Action run (foundry) over time.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank: (1) Segment the ledger: rolling windowed log files (frames-<epoch>.jsonl) with a small always-fresh latest.json pointer + a manifest of segments; readers fetch only the tail segment + manifest, never the whole history. (2) Snapshot+prune: periodically checkpoint state to a signed compact snapshot and archive old segments out of the served path (Releases assets, or external object store) keeping each served file well under 100MB. (3) Track served-file size as a foundry health metric with an alarm at 50MB. (4) Use Git LFS or external storage for anything large; never let a served JSON grow unbounded.
wrenches.residual_risk: Segmentation works but adds a manifest/consistency surface (which segment is current is itself a CDN-staleness problem, see wrench 1). History pruned out of the served path is no longer cheaply verifiable by light clients.

wrenches.title: Issues-as-ledger throughput is capped at ~one account's rate limit regardless of swarm size — it cannot scale to millions of edges
wrenches.scenario: Primary REST limit is ~5000 req/hr/account (15000 for GitHub Apps/Enterprise on some paths), and secondary content-creation limits are far lower. A million edges each writing telemetry even once per hour is ~1M writes/hr against a 5000/hr ceiling. The only ways out — account farms or many tokens — are themselves AUP violations and trip abuse detection. The ledger's write throughput does not grow with the fleet; it is pinned to a single account's budget.
wrenches.impact: Hard scaling ceiling: 'planetary' is throughput-bounded to roughly one org's worth of writes. Past a few hundred chatty edges the system either drops telemetry, falls minutes-to-hours behind, or gets throttled into a ban (wrench 2).
wrenches.mitigation: Bank: (1) Hierarchical aggregation: edges report to regional aggregators/twins; only aggregators write to the ledger, collapsing N edges into O(regions) writes. (2) Coalesce + batch: one append per edge per window, sized to stay under secondary limits; adaptive cadence under backpressure. (3) Move bulk telemetry off the rate-limited Issues path entirely onto an append-friendly object store behind the Transport interface; keep Issues (if at all) for low-rate control events only. (4) Capacity-model the ledger explicitly and publish the supported edges x cadence envelope.
wrenches.residual_risk: Aggregation reintroduces a hierarchy/SPOF at each aggregator and adds latency; the architecture's clean 'every edge writes to GitHub directly' story doesn't survive scale and must be reframed.

wrenches.title: The frame foundry is a GitHub Action — Actions outages/quotas/scheduler-drift freeze NEW echos for the WHOLE fleet at once (degrade-to-one doesn't cover correlated foundry loss)
wrenches.scenario: Echos are forged per-edge by a scheduled GitHub Action. Actions is among GitHub's most frequently degraded services; scheduled workflows are best-effort and routinely delayed many minutes (or skipped) under platform load; monthly minute quotas and concurrency caps apply, and a billing lapse cuts Actions off. O(N) per-edge forging exhausts minutes or queues at scale. When the foundry stalls, NO new echos are forged for ANY edge simultaneously — a correlated failure the 'degrade-to-one' story (which only covers a single edge losing its link) does not address.
wrenches.impact: Fleet-wide guidance staleness from one component's outage: every edge freezes on its last echo at the same time, and telemetry backs up with nothing consuming it. The control loop's clock is GitHub's best-effort scheduler, so even nominally it has unpredictable tick jitter.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank: (1) Make the foundry portable (plain container/process) so it can run off-GitHub (a tiny VM, a function, on-prem) — Actions is one host, not the design. (2) Run a hot standby foundry on independent infra with leader election so a GitHub-Actions outage fails over. (3) Idempotent, resumable forging with a persisted cursor so a skipped/queued run catches up without double-forging (also addresses the latest.json race below). (4) Decouple cadence from the Actions cron: forge on telemetry-arrival events where possible, and publish a foundry-health heartbeat so edges can tell 'my echo is old because the foundry is down' vs 'because my link is down' and degrade appropriately. (5) Budget/alarm on Actions minutes.
wrenches.residual_risk: A standby foundry adds split-brain/leader-election complexity and its own key-custody surface; even portable, the per-edge O(N) forging cost still grows with the fleet and bounds tick cadence at scale.

wrenches.title: Copilot token + sessions are plaintext on disk with no Purview/DLP, eDiscovery, or in-tenant audit
wrenches.scenario: Auth chain caches credentials in .copilot_token / .copilot_session as plaintext files; agent conversations and memory (ManageMemory/ContextMemory) persist locally under .brainstem_data with no sensitivity labeling, retention, legal-hold, or eDiscovery surface, and no immutable in-tenant audit log of agent actions. Compliance asks 'show me every action this agent took and prove the log is tamper-evident' and there is no answer inside the tenant.
wrenches.impact: Fails Purview governance and SOX/FINRA/legal-hold requirements; plaintext token theft enables identity replay; no non-repudiation for autonomous actions. Discovery/retention obligations cannot be met.
wrenches.mitigation: Store secrets via OS keychain / Key Vault, never plaintext. Emit a tamper-evident, append-only audit of every tool invocation to an in-tenant sink (Sentinel/Log Analytics). Integrate Purview for sensitivity labels, DLP, retention, and eDiscovery over agent conversations/memory/objects. Provide a per-agent action ledger keyed to its Entra Agent ID.
wrenches.residual_risk: Local-first edges (especially offline frame-net nodes) buffer audit data that can be lost before sync; retrofitting Purview onto file-based memory is partial.

wrenches.title: No Defender/XDR visibility — compromised or rogue agents are invisible to the SOC
wrenches.scenario: Agent actions (file writes, shell, network, tool-calls) generate no Defender/Sentinel telemetry. A compromised cartridge, a prompt-injected memory, or a rogue edge operates with zero detection signal. There is no kill-switch the SOC can pull, and the frame-net's 'degrade-to-one keeps thinking on its last echo' means an isolated compromised edge keeps acting autonomously with no recall.
wrenches.impact: SOC cannot detect, investigate, or contain an agent incident; violates the assume-breach posture Microsoft sells. The autonomy-on-stale-echo behavior is an attacker's dream: cut the link, keep the compromised edge running on a poisoned echo.
wrenches.mitigation: Ship a Defender/MDE sensor hook and Sentinel connector emitting agent-action and anomaly events; define detections (unexpected shell agent, mass file egress, echo-source anomaly). Add a signed kill-switch / quarantine echo that an edge MUST honor before acting, plus a max-staleness 'safe mode' that drops to read-only when an echo ages out instead of continuing to act.
wrenches.residual_risk: Kill-switch delivery is bounded by link latency on far/intermittent edges; safe-mode-on-staleness reduces capability exactly when an operator might legitimately need autonomy.

wrenches.title: Echo/frame poisoning: signed-but-malicious guidance steers the edge LLM into harmful action (stored prompt injection)
wrenches.scenario: Echos are forged FROM telemetry and consumed as guidance the edge's LLM reconciles against local reality. If an attacker can write telemetry (GitHub Issues API is append-only and broadly writable) or compromise the frame-foundry Action, they inject content that the edge LLM treats as trusted aim. Even a correctly-signed echo can carry adversarial natural-language that jailbreaks the local model. ManageMemory makes this persistent across sessions.
wrenches.impact: Signing authenticates the courier, not the message — a legitimately-signed echo full of prompt-injection still hijacks the edge's judgment. With memory persistence it becomes a durable, self-reinforcing compromise that survives restarts.
wrenches.mitigation: Treat all frame/echo/telemetry content as untrusted data, never instructions: strict structured schemas (typed fields, no free-form imperative text into the system prompt), provenance-tagging of every guidance fragment, and a constrained reconciler that cannot escalate privilege from guidance alone. Quarantine + human-review echos that would trigger high-impact actions. Sign the foundry's inputs, not just its outputs.
wrenches.residual_risk: LLM injection is not fully solvable; structured guidance still influences behavior; a sufficiently clever payload inside allowed fields can nudge outcomes.

wrenches.title: Frame-foundry GitHub Action is CI/CD-as-the-forge — workflow compromise = forge echos for the whole fleet
wrenches.scenario: A GitHub Action forges per-edge echos. Actions are a well-known supply-chain target: PR-triggered workflow injection, secret exfiltration via pull_request_target, a poisoned third-party action in the chain, or branch-protection bypass. Compromise the foundry and you mint trusted guidance for every edge — fleet takeover via CI, no endpoint exploit needed.
wrenches.impact: Single-point fleet compromise through a pipeline most reviewers won't scrutinize as a control plane. Echos are signed by the foundry's key, so downstream verification passes.
wrenches.mitigation: Harden the foundry like a production signing service: isolated, least-privilege runner; OIDC short-lived creds not stored secrets; pinned action SHAs; no pull_request_target on untrusted input; branch protection + required reviews; signing key in HSM/Key Vault with attestation; reproducible, logged forge runs. Better: move forging off public Actions into the governed enterprise substrate.
wrenches.residual_risk: HSM-signed forge still trusts its input telemetry (see poisoning wrench); insider with foundry access can forge; GitHub-platform compromise is out of customer control.

wrenches.title: Security controls implemented as agents are bypassable — no mandatory, non-removable enforcement point
wrenches.scenario: 'Everything is an agent, agents load/unload freely.' If auth, DLP, audit, or policy enforcement are themselves agents, an attacker (or a careless user) can delete/disable them via the unauthenticated /agents DELETE route or by not installing them, and the control silently vanishes. There is no fail-closed policy enforcement point that cannot be removed.
wrenches.impact: Defense-in-depth becomes opt-out. A reviewer demands 'show me the control that CANNOT be bypassed'; if the answer is 'it's an agent you can delete', the model fails. Kernel agents exist (KERNEL_AGENTS) but the principle isn't a security boundary.
wrenches.mitigation: Define a fail-closed policy kernel: identity, authz, audit emission, and DLP gating live in the protected core (not in removable agents) and the request path refuses to serve if they're absent or tampered. Make the audit/identity hooks non-bypassable preconditions of /chat and the Leviathan wire, attested at startup.
wrenches.residual_risk: Expanding the protected core grows the frozen-kernel surface and the kernel-lock tension; attestation can be spoofed on a fully-compromised host.

wrenches.title: GitHub is an unSLA'd, ToS-constrained single point of failure for the entire control/transport plane
wrenches.scenario: READ via raw.githubusercontent.com, WRITE via Issues API, forge via Actions — all GitHub. A GitHub outage, rate-limit, Actions-quota exhaustion, or an Acceptable-Use enforcement action (using raw as a CDN/DB and Issues as a message bus arguably violates GitHub AUP) takes the planetary control plane offline or gets the org's account throttled/banned. Microsoft would be selling enterprises a control plane that depends on a public service it does not SLA for that purpose.
wrenches.impact: Availability and ToS risk: the swarm 'degrade-to-one' saves individual edges but the coordination/forge/canon layer is down or banned; no enterprise SLA backs it. AUP enforcement could nuke the whole deployment's account.
wrenches.mitigation: Get explicit AUP sign-off or move to GitHub Enterprise / Azure-native substrate with real SLAs for enterprise tier; implement rate-limit backoff, multi-region/mirror read fallback, and a conditional-on-foundry-availability mode. Treat public GitHub transport as best-effort community tier, not the enterprise control plane.
wrenches.residual_risk: Even GitHub Enterprise shares platform risk; mirroring adds consistency complexity to the content-addressed chain.

wrenches.title: Multi-tenant data bleed through shared canonical twins, frames, and community agents
wrenches.scenario: The canonical twin is 'globally-public, content-addressed' and frames are 'swarm-shared'. If enterprise edges read/write the same shared objects and registry (RAR) as the community, one tenant's data or a malicious cross-tenant agent can flow through shared GitHub objects. There is no tenant isolation boundary in the frame-net; content-addressing dedupes across tenants by design.
wrenches.impact: Cross-tenant leakage and a confused-deputy path: tenant A's agent ingests tenant B's 'shared' frame/agent; a poisoned community object lands in an enterprise via the shared canon. Multi-tenancy isolation is table-stakes for any Microsoft service.
wrenches.mitigation: Hard tenant-scoped namespaces with no implicit cross-tenant object sharing; enterprise canon/frames live in a tenant-bound substrate; explicit, reviewed import to bring a community object across the boundary (with re-signing under tenant authority); never auto-resolve shared content-addresses across tenant lines.
wrenches.residual_risk: Content-addressing's dedupe value is reduced by isolation; the community<->enterprise import gate is a recurring social-engineering target.

wrenches.title: Stored prompt-injection via memory + the 3-round tool loop = injection-to-action with no human in the loop
wrenches.scenario: /chat auto-injects <memory> context and runs an up-to-3-round tool loop that executes agent perform() methods. ManageMemory persists content. An attacker plants injected instructions in a document the agent reads or in memory; on a later turn the LLM follows them and invokes a shell/file/network agent. EU AI Act 'high-risk + human oversight' expectations are unmet because the edge acts on local judgment with no in-tenant audit or human gate.
wrenches.impact: Untrusted-content-to-privileged-action chain, made persistent and autonomous. Accountability/liability gap: who is responsible when an unsupervised edge takes a harmful real-world action on injected guidance? Regulatory exposure on top of the security one.
wrenches.mitigation: Privilege-separate the tool loop: data-plane content can never directly authorize high-impact tools; require explicit human-approval (or a signed policy allow) for irreversible/high-impact actions; taint-track memory provenance and never elevate from tainted memory; cap and log every tool round to the in-tenant audit; provide a human-oversight mode mappable to AI Act obligations.
wrenches.residual_risk: Approval fatigue pushes users to auto-approve; taint-tracking is incomplete; low-impact-but-aggregated actions can still cause harm below the approval threshold.

wrenches.title: GitHub as transport = someone else's kill-switch, ToS, rate-limiter, and legal department
wrenches.scenario: The 'no server' romance hides that the entire planetary transport is ONE company's infrastructure under ToS. Microsoft/GitHub can rate-limit, flag-for-abuse, suspend the account, or be legally compelled to take down the repo. A swarm of thousands of edges hammering the Issues API for telemetry trips GitHub's secondary rate limits → GitHub's own anti-abuse becomes a swarm-wide DoS. raw.githubusercontent has a multi-minute CDN cache, so the 'cheap heartbeat' can be silently STALE by minutes even when freshly written — and an attacker who knows the TTL can time actions to the stale window.
wrenches.impact: Availability of the whole frame-net is at the mercy of a third party's abuse heuristics and policy, with no SLA and no failover. 'Resilient because serverless' is actually 'fragile because single-vendor.'
wrenches.mitigation: Treat GitHub as ONE transport behind an abstraction, not THE transport — define a transport interface so frames/echos/telemetry can also flow over S3/IPFS/plain HTTPS/a relay; budget and back off to respect rate limits (batch telemetry, exponential backoff, jitter); design for the CDN TTL explicitly (freshness/expiry in the signed pointer); keep mirror repos/orgs ready; have a legal/ToS contingency for takedown.
wrenches.residual_risk: A second transport doubles the attack surface and the operational burden; CDN staleness is inherent and only papered over by expiry semantics, not eliminated.

wrenches.title: 'Append-only telemetry via Issues API' is neither append-only nor authenticated-write — it's a public spam/poison/edit surface
wrenches.scenario: Anyone with a GitHub account can open issues on a public repo. The foundry ingests issues as edge telemetry; without per-edge crypto binding, attacker-authored issues ARE telemetry. Even with signing: (a) issues and comments are EDITABLE and DELETABLE — 'append-only' is false; an attacker (or compromised maintainer) edits an issue body AFTER the foundry's audit assumed immutability; (b) flood the repo with millions of issues → the foundry Action times out / burns Action minutes / costs money / the repo gets abuse-flagged → telemetry-channel DoS.
wrenches.impact: Unauthenticated injection into the telemetry pipeline, a false immutability assumption baked into the audit trail, and a cheap economic/availability DoS against the foundry — all from a free GitHub account.
wrenches.mitigation: Require every telemetry issue to be signed by a known edge key and reject all others at ingest (don't trust GitHub identity); capture and pin the exact bytes+hash the foundry read so later edits can't rewrite history (store the read snapshot in the signed chain); rate-limit/allowlist which accounts can write telemetry (or use a write-restricted private repo with a relay); make the foundry resilient to issue floods (pre-filter by signature before doing any expensive work, hard caps, dedup).
wrenches.residual_risk: Signature-gating telemetry pushes the trust onto edge key management (see HMAC/revocation wrenches); a private-repo relay reintroduces a server the architecture wanted to avoid.

wrenches.title: Twin squatting + TOFU first-contact: register a Mars edge's twin before it ever phones home
wrenches.scenario: Others reach an edge THROUGH its public twin (twin.json identity + verify key, discovered by convention like org/edge-name). A new edge hasn't reported in yet (it's en route / pre-deployment / blacked out). An attacker creates a repo/path that looks like that edge's twin, with a key THEY minted, and gets the swarm directory/index to point at it. Trust-on-first-use without an out-of-band root means the attacker IS the edge as far as the swarm is concerned — and the real edge, on first contact, finds its identity already claimed.
wrenches.impact: Identity hijack of an edge before it exists in the network; siblings send echos/messages to and trust telemetry from the impostor; the legitimate edge is locked out or forced into a confusing split-identity.
wrenches.mitigation: Pre-provision each edge's identity out-of-band BEFORE deployment: its public key and canonical twin path are registered in the SIGNED canonical index at manufacture/launch time, so there is no TOFU window. Bind twin discovery to that signed registry, not to naming convention. Make the edge's verify key the join key, not its GitHub path.
wrenches.residual_risk: Pre-provisioning needs a secure manufacturing/launch ceremony; a lost-and-replaced edge needs a re-provisioning path that itself can be abused.

wrenches.title: Twin inbox = unauthenticated command mailbox + precious-bandwidth DoS
wrenches.scenario: The public twin has an inbox/ mailbox of echos+messages that 'others reach the edge through.' If any sibling (or anyone who can write the inbox path) can queue messages, and the edge drains its inbox on contact and feeds it to its LLM, then attacker-authored inbox messages get processed during the edge's brief, expensive contact window. A cleverly-worded 'message' exploits LLM helpfulness even though spec says guidance != commands. Separately: flood the inbox so the edge burns its entire Mars-link bandwidth draining spam instead of exchanging real telemetry/echos.
wrenches.impact: Unauthenticated semantic injection into an autonomous edge plus a bandwidth-exhaustion DoS where every wasted byte is enormously costly and the contact window is irreplaceable.
wrenches.mitigation: Inbox writes must be signed by allowlisted senders only; the edge fetches a signed manifest of inbox contents and pulls only verified items, hard-capped by count/size, prioritized (foundry echo > known sibling > everything dropped); inbox messages are typed data, never instructions, and run through the same below-LLM guardrail as frames; budget contact-window bandwidth so spam can never crowd out telemetry.
wrenches.residual_risk: Allowlist management across a growing swarm is hard; a compromised allowlisted sibling still gets through; prioritization heuristics can be gamed to starve legitimate low-priority messages.

wrenches.title: Replay of public telemetry + tick rollback across reboot/clock-skew (Mars has no NTP)
wrenches.scenario: Telemetry issues are public and signed — anyone can read and re-POST an OLD valid telemetry record. The foundry forges an echo from 'last telemetry' and re-applies guidance for a world that no longer exists. Monotonic tick is the usual defense, but on an edge with no reliable clock, a reboot/power-cycle/state-wipe can reset the high-water mark, and a wrong clock makes timestamp-based freshness unreliable — letting a genuinely-signed old frame re-apply as 'current.'
wrenches.impact: Replay that passes all signature checks, made worse by the edge's inability to keep monotonic state across reboots or trust its own clock — the foundry and edge can be walked backward in time.
wrenches.mitigation: Enforce strict tick monotonicity at BOTH foundry and edge, persisted in tamper-resistant local storage that survives reboot; nonce/per-record uniqueness so an exact replay is detectably duplicate; bind freshness to chain position (prev-hash + tick) rather than wall-clock; on detecting a backward tick, degrade to safe local-only mode rather than obeying. Don't trust edge wall-clock for security decisions.
wrenches.residual_risk: An attacker who can wipe the edge's persisted high-water mark resets the ratchet; nonce stores grow unbounded and need pruning windows that themselves create replay gaps.

wrenches.title: Downgrade & algorithm-confusion at the Ed25519 migration — the public key becomes an HMAC forging secret
wrenches.scenario: Migration means frames carry an alg field (HMAC-SHA256 vs Ed25519 vs rapp-moment ECDSA) and edges accept both during the cutover. Classic JWT-style alg-confusion: the Ed25519 verify key is PUBLIC; an attacker takes that public key, uses it as the HMAC SECRET, and emits a frame with alg=HMAC-SHA256. A verifier that selects its verification routine from the message's own alg field will HMAC-verify using the public key and PASS. Simpler still: alg=none, or just keep emitting HMAC frames during the 'we still accept both' window and forge freely (the HMAC secret is everywhere, per wrench 2). Intermittent edges make the flag-day impossible to coordinate — some edges won't have received the cutover frame and will accept HMAC for months.
wrenches.impact: Forgery straight through the migration; the move TO real crypto becomes the window where crypto is weakest.
wrenches.mitigation: Never let the message choose its own verification algorithm. Pin the expected alg per-key in twin.json/the key record (a key is bound to exactly one alg); reject any alg mismatch and alg=none. Treat the Ed25519 public key as strictly a verify key that can NEVER be used as a MAC secret (separate key material, separate domain tag). Make HMAC removal a hard cutoff keyed to a signed flag-day tick, with edges that miss it failing CLOSED rather than continuing to accept HMAC.
wrenches.residual_risk: Coordinating a hard cutover across edges that may be offline for the whole migration window guarantees a long tail of stragglers; those either lose guidance (availability hit) or must be granted an unsafe grace period.

wrenches.title: Telemetry poisoning -> confused-deputy echo: crypto verifies the SIGNER, never the INPUT
wrenches.scenario: Echos are forged FROM each edge's last telemetry, written append-only via the GitHub Issues API. If telemetry writes aren't edge-authenticated, an attacker opens issues impersonating edge A's outbox (or writes fake telemetry into A's channel). The foundry ingests the lie and forges a poisoned echo 'for A's own good,' then signs it CORRECTLY. A verifies the foundry signature, it passes, and A acts on guidance derived from attacker-controlled inputs. The poison entered UPSTREAM of the signature — the crypto faithfully attests a corrupted computation. The same applies to the Dream Catcher 'contradiction' doctrine: contradictory frames are KEPT as 'alternate-dimension data', so injected false telemetry/frames are absorbed by design, not rejected.
wrenches.impact: Whole-edge manipulation that is cryptographically 'valid' end-to-end; the signer-authenticity guarantee actively LAUNDERS the attack into looking authoritative.
wrenches.mitigation: Authenticate telemetry at the source: every outbox entry signed by the EDGE's own key; the foundry rejects unsigned/wrong-key/wrong-edge telemetry. Echos must record the telemetry HASHES they were derived from, so the edge can cross-check 'this echo was forged from frames I actually emitted.' For the contradiction doctrine, require a valid signature before a contradiction is admitted as 'alternate-dimension data' — unsigned contradictions are dropped, not preserved.
wrenches.residual_risk: The foundry remains a high-value confused-deputy (compromise it and all echos are poisoned at the source); a compromised edge can still feed truthful-looking-but-wrong telemetry about itself.

wrenches.title: No revocation/rotation story on an append-only, intermittent net — and the thing you'd sign the revocation with is the key you're revoking
wrenches.scenario: When (not if) the foundry/edge key is compromised, how do you tell thousands of intermittent edges to STOP trusting it? Revocation must reach edges that are by definition hard to reach, and the revocation message must itself be trusted — but the only key the edges currently trust may be the very one you're revoking (chicken-and-egg). An edge running on its last echo for 40 days has no channel to learn its trust anchor is dead, and append-only chains can't 'un-say' a signature.
wrenches.impact: A single key compromise is effectively permanent and unrecoverable across the long tail of offline edges; forged-but-valid guidance keeps verifying indefinitely.
wrenches.mitigation: Design the key hierarchy NOW, before pilot: a long-lived OFFLINE ROOT key (air-gapped, quorum/multi-party, signs ONLY delegation+rotation certs) that delegates to short-lived OPERATIONAL signing keys. Edges trust the root; accept operational keys via signed, EXPIRING delegations. Rotation = root signs a new operational key and a CRL/serial-revocation embedded in latest/frame. Crucially, give operational keys a hard EXPIRY so a compromised key self-expires even if the revocation never reaches a given edge.
wrenches.residual_risk: Root-key compromise is unrecoverable unless a backup root + quorum is pre-provisioned at manufacture; long-offline edges still act on a key that may have been revoked until its expiry catches up; expiry forces re-issuance cadence the net may not sustain.

wrenches.title: twin.json TOFU — who signs the twin's verify key? The PKI bottoms out in 'who can write this GitHub repo path'
wrenches.scenario: Every edge has a public twin whose twin.json carries {identity + verify key}, and peers reach the edge 'through its twin.' But twin.json is just a file in a GitHub repo. If a peer trusts whatever key it first finds there (Trust-On-First-Use), an attacker who controls that repo path at provisioning time, merges a malicious PR, or compromises the repo, SWAPS the verify key. From then on the attacker's 'signed' state.json/echos verify perfectly against the swapped key. L2 identity (rapp-rappid/2.0) is a UUIDv4 'content-addressed in a public file, no central registry' — i.e. also TOFU. The cryptography is solid; the trust simply bottoms out in GitHub ACLs, not keys.
wrenches.impact: Full identity takeover of an edge's public front door; all downstream signature checks become attacker-controlled. Defeats verify-before-act by corrupting the verify KEY rather than the signature.
wrenches.mitigation: Bind each edge's identity to a key FINGERPRINT published in an independent root-of-trust (the rapp-god canonical registry / a transparency log / a keypair-bound rappid), not to twin.json's self-asserted key. Peers pin A's key by fingerprint from that registry; any key change must be a signed key-rotation cert chaining from the OLD key (no silent swaps). Protect the twin repo path with required reviews + CODEOWNERS so a lone PR can't rewrite identity.
wrenches.residual_risk: The registry becomes the meta-root with the same spoofing question one level up (turtles) — so the registry's own anchor needs the offline-root + transparency-log treatment, and genuine key loss (no old key to sign rotation) needs an out-of-band recovery path that is itself attackable.

wrenches.title: Split-brain: an implicit distributed-consensus system with no consensus protocol
wrenches.scenario: Frames are prev-hash-chained, append-only, swarm-shared, with many edges writing telemetry and a foundry forging. That is an implicit distributed log with no stated consistency model. Two edges can fork the chain (both extend tick N); late/out-of-order telemetry merges wrong; one edge acts on frame N while the swarm is at N+5; different edges hold different 'latest' and mis-coordinate (both grab the same task, or both back off).
wrenches.impact: Coordinated actions collide or leave gaps; double-work; the 'one being' fractures into inconsistent limbs.
wrenches.mitigation: State the model explicitly: eventually-consistent, single-writer-foundry, last-echo-wins per edge. Design tasks to be partition-tolerant — CRDT-style claims, leases with TTL, idempotent and commutative actions, nothing that requires global agreement to be SAFE. Foundry is the single linearizer with a monotonic tick. Every action must be safe under 'I may be N frames behind.' Detect forks via prev-hash mismatch → conservative mode.
wrenches.residual_risk: Single foundry is an availability bottleneck/SPOF; eventual consistency forbids whole classes of tightly-coordinated tasks; lease TTLs are coarse under light-delay.

wrenches.title: Edge clock is the unguarded root of every freshness/crypto assumption
wrenches.scenario: All TTL, expiry, valid-until, revocation-epoch, and freshness decisions depend on the edge knowing the time. A disconnected edge has no NTP; clocks drift over days, and a compromised edge can spoof its own clock — making expired echos look fresh (or fresh ones look dead). On space edges, time sync is genuinely hard. One clock attack defeats W2, W3, W8, and W14 simultaneously.
wrenches.impact: A single unprotected dependency that silently invalidates the entire freshness-based safety model.
wrenches.mitigation: Use frame TICKS (the foundry's monotonic logical clock) as the PRIMARY ordering, not wall-clock — 'I am N ticks behind' is robust to drift. Where wall-clock is unavoidable, use signed timestamps + bounded-drift assumptions + a hardware RTC and cross-check multiple time sources on contact. Degrade to conservative behavior when time is UNCERTAIN ('if I can't trust my clock, I act conservatively').
wrenches.residual_risk: Logical ticks don't map to physical urgency (some actions have wall-clock deadlines); RTC adds cost; a fully compromised edge's clock can't be trusted at all.

wrenches.title: 'No server' hides the real SPOF: the single frame foundry and its forge key in Action secrets
wrenches.scenario: The frame foundry IS a server — a single GitHub Action in one repo holding the forge signing key as a secret, with write access across twins. If the Action breaks, is misconfigured, is rate-limited, or the repo is suspended, NO edge gets fresh echos. If the forge key leaks from Action secrets, an attacker forges swarm-wide (compounds W7). A compromised foundry = swarm RCE-by-guidance.
wrenches.impact: Centralized failure and centralized compromise behind a 'serverless' label; forge-key theft is game-over for the planet.
wrenches.mitigation: Treat the foundry as the trust-critical control plane and harden it: forge key in an HSM or OIDC-federated short-lived signing (not a long-lived Action secret), key rotation, reproducible/attestable Action builds, per-twin scoped write permissions, and M-of-N independent foundries (multi-signature) for high-assurance swarms. Make the bounded survival window explicit (edge degrade-to-one + TTL survives ONE foundry-outage window) and monitor foundry liveness as a first-class signal.
wrenches.residual_risk: M-of-N foundries add latency/complexity; GitHub Actions supply-chain risk remains; survival is only for a bounded window.

wrenches.title: Conflicting telemetry from many edges — merge/conflict-resolution policy is undefined and manipulable
wrenches.scenario: Many edges report overlapping, contradictory observations of a shared world (two rovers report different positions for the same target) that the foundry must fold into swarm-shared frames. With no defined conflict-resolution (CRDT / quorum / trust-weighting), the default is last-writer-wins by issue arrival order — which is arbitrary AND attacker-influenceable: just be the last or loudest writer to set the swarm-shared 'truth'.
wrenches.impact: The shared world-model becomes whatever the noisiest or most-recent edge asserts; a single (possibly compromised) edge steers fleet-wide beliefs and thus everyone's echos.
wrenches.mitigation: Define an explicit, deterministic conflict-resolution policy: trust/reputation-weighted merge, quorum or sensor-fusion for shared facts, and outlier rejection. PRESERVE PROVENANCE in the frame (which edge asserted what, with what confidence) rather than silently collapsing to one value, so the LLM-reconciler at each edge can weigh disputed facts itself. Mark contested facts as 'disputed' instead of arbitrarily picking. Reputation decays for edges whose assertions are repeatedly refuted.
wrenches.residual_risk: Reputation/quorum systems are themselves Sybil-attackable (wrench #4) and slow to react to a newly-compromised previously-trusted edge; some genuine ground-truth conflicts have no automatable resolution and need human adjudication.

wrenches.title: Eventual-consistency lag -> edges take contradictory coordinated actions simultaneously
wrenches.scenario: Per-PoP CDN caching and link delay mean two edges coordinating a joint action are each acting on a DIFFERENT frame generation at the same wall-clock moment. Each computes its role from its own stale view: both think they're leader, or both defer (livelock), or they take physically incompatible steps. This is classic distributed coordination over a lossy, high-latency, no-consensus medium.
wrenches.impact: Coordination failures with physical consequences: collisions, deadlock/livelock, duplicated or dropped joint actions — the hardest class to debug because every edge was individually 'correct' on its own view.
wrenches.mitigation: Do NOT require synchronous consensus over this wire. Design guidance to be asynchronous, idempotent, and self-stabilizing (aim points and invariants, not step-by-step deltas). Encode 'valid-from tick / superseded-by' so an edge knows its frame may be stale and acts CONSERVATIVELY when uncertain. Exclusive/joint actions use leases or tokens carried in echos with explicit expiry (a lease that lapses defaults to the safe state). Safety-critical interlocks live LOCALLY at the edge, never frame-coordinated alone.
wrenches.residual_risk: Leases over days of light-delay must be long, so an edge holding a stale lease can still act while a peer waits; self-stabilization converges eventually but the transient window can be long enough to matter physically.

wrenches.title: Sybil edge flood via free, unlimited GitHub accounts
wrenches.scenario: Trust bootstrap gives any new edge a public twin by creating a GitHub identity + repo + self-signed key. GitHub accounts are free and unlimited. An attacker spins up 10,000 'edges,' each with a valid twin.json and verify key. If any reputation, frame authority, voting, eviction, or 'swarm consensus' weights by edge count, the attacker owns those mechanisms. Even if it doesn't, the foundry now must forge echos for 10k phantom edges and the Issues/raw API rate limits get exhausted. The twins are content-addressed and validly self-signed, so they all look legitimate — signature is not identity.
wrenches.impact: Governance capture (vote/quorum/reputation games), or a cheap DoS of the foundry and substrate. Undermines any 'the swarm decides' mechanism that assumes one-edge-one-voice.
wrenches.mitigation: Decouple identity from keypair: a new edge must be vouched/co-signed by an existing quorum (web-of-trust) or anchored to a scarce, attestable root (Entra Agent ID via Agent 365, hardware attestation, or an economic stake) before it counts. Sybil-resistant reputation = vouching graph, not headcount. Rate-limit and admission-control the foundry per namespace so phantom edges cost the attacker, not the swarm.
wrenches.residual_risk: Web-of-trust raises the onboarding bar (collides with frictionless adoption); a patient attacker can still farm vouches or stake to build a smaller, legitimate-looking sybil set.

wrenches.title: First-contact trust for a brand-new edge — the bootstrap paradox where the first echo defines the worldview
wrenches.scenario: A new edge has no reputation and no pinned key anywhere, yet must be trusted enough to receive echos and have its telemetry believed. Too open => that's the sybil problem. Too closed => the legit Mars edge cannot onboard without the human round-trip the architecture exists to avoid. The sharp edge: the FIRST echo an edge ever receives establishes its worldview, and if delivered by an impostor twin during the unpinned window, the edge is born compromised. TOFU is dangerous precisely when 'first use' is attacker-reachable.
wrenches.impact: An edge that is corrupted at birth never had a clean state to fall back to; degrade-to-one means it degrades to a poisoned baseline. Either onboarding is exploitable, or it is so locked-down it defeats the autonomy premise.
wrenches.mitigation: Enrollment ceremony: a new edge ships with an operator/manufacturer-signed birth certificate (key endorsed by an offline root or the Agent 365 control plane) BEFORE it ever accepts a frame. Pin-by-endorsement, not pin-on-first-contact. Quorum vouching to admit to the swarm. Probationary trust: a new edge's telemetry is low-weight and its actions bounded until it accrues verified, cross-checked history. Make the birth certificate the content-addressed record in the canonical registry.
wrenches.residual_risk: A compromised provisioning/manufacturing step backdoors the birth certificate before it ever ships; probationary periods slow legitimate scale-up.

wrenches.title: No abuse/eviction governance — a captured or malfunctioning edge cannot be cleanly expelled at planetary scale
wrenches.scenario: At planetary scale you WILL get a rogue, captured, or malfunctioning edge: spamming telemetry, publishing junk to its outbox, or (if it holds any frame-authority share) poisoning peers. There is no described mechanism for who decides to evict and how that decision propagates over a one-way wire. Append-only + serverless means you cannot just delete the bad actor. And governance itself is capturable: if the deciders are few, compromise them; if eviction is by vote, sybil breaks it.
wrenches.impact: An attacker-held edge persists indefinitely as a poison source with no off-switch, or eviction power is centralized into a new single point of compromise. Either way the swarm cannot self-heal.
wrenches.mitigation: Make a signed allow/deny 'swarm roster' a first-class, versioned, content-addressed artifact (like the frame chain) that edges fetch and ENFORCE in their verify-before-act path. Eviction = a quorum-signed roster update with an epoch bump; edges honor the last-known roster even when degraded-to-one. Publish a bounded governance process (who proposes/seconds/ratifies). Add reputation-decay and rate limits so a quiet-then-loud edge self-throttles before human governance even engages.
wrenches.residual_risk: Roster propagation lag means an evicted edge keeps acting until the update reaches its peers; quorum custody for the roster is itself a capture target.

wrenches.title: GitHub is the planet's single point of failure, control, and censorship — an authority root you didn't choose
wrenches.scenario: The entire trust+transport substrate is one company's product: raw.githubusercontent.com (read), Issues API (write), Actions (foundry). A GitHub outage, secondary-rate-limit trip, or abuse-detection flag can silence the whole swarm at once. GitHub — or a government acting through it — can suspend the org and instantly cut every edge from guidance and telemetry. Running an automated planetary command fleet on Issues plausibly violates acceptable-use and gets the org banned. Export/sanctions geofencing on the CDN can partition edges by region.
wrenches.impact: A non-technical actor (GitHub policy, a court order, an abuse heuristic) becomes the ultimate kill-switch and root of authority over a system marketed as resilient and server-less. ToS violation could erase the substrate overnight.
wrenches.mitigation: Abstract transport behind a signed, content-addressed interface so GitHub is ONE backend among several (IPFS / S3 / other static hosts) — the verify chain must depend only on signatures, never on GitHub being authentic. Mirror frames/echos to a second substrate. Explicitly treat GitHub as an untrusted CDN that cannot forge signed frames. Define a 'GitHub down/hostile' degrade path (edges already degrade-to-one; ensure re-acquisition via an alternate front door). Vet ToS proactively; in the Agent 365 pitch, position Entra as the authority root and GitHub as mere transport.
wrenches.residual_risk: Multi-substrate adds operational and consistency complexity; until mirrors exist, a single ToS action is still fatal, and the alternate hosts carry their own censorship/availability risks.

wrenches.title: Hostile fork of the canonical foundation — drift detection turned against itself because canonicity is a URL+hash, not a signed root
wrenches.scenario: Every spec has a public canonical twin, content-addressed over raw.githubusercontent.com; rapp-god detects drift vs the canonical hash. Attack: fork the canonical repo to `evil/rapp-spec`, relax a safety invariant / insert a backdoored kernel-invariant / point frame-authority at the attacker's key, then convince some edges or installers that the FORK is canonical — via a poisoned install one-liner, a typo-squatted org, SEO/social, or a compromised landing page. Now drift detection PASSES against the wrong canon: the attacker's edges read as 'compliant' and the real ones look 'drifted.' Canonicity is just a URL plus a hash with no signature binding the hash to a trusted authority. The install one-liner is curl|bash from a path that can be MITM'd or typo-squatted.
wrenches.impact: Mass adoption of a backdoored 'canonical' foundation; the integrity tool (rapp-god) actively endorses the attacker's version. Undermines the one invariant the whole ecosystem leans on — that there is a single trustworthy canon.
wrenches.mitigation: Root canonicity cryptographically, not by URL: sign the canonical hash-set with an offline foundation root key shipped pinned with the kernel, so rapp-god verifies 'this hash is endorsed by THE foundation,' not 'this matches the URL I was handed.' Key-transparency log for canon updates (multi-party, anti-rollback). The SACRED install one-liner must verify a signature, not merely curl|bash a path. Actively detect and publicly flag forks claiming canonicity.
wrenches.residual_risk: The pinned root key shipped with the kernel becomes a high-value target and a rotation headache; users who bypass verification (curl|bash habit) remain exposed.

wrenches.title: An LLM as the edge's decision authority is non-deterministic and uncertifiable for any safety-critical or regulated edge — and silent model-version swaps change safety behavior with no redeploy
wrenches.scenario: The frame-net thesis is 'the edge is a full brainstem making LOCAL JUDGMENTS; its own LLM reconciles guidance vs reality and decides.' For the flagship physical edges (a Mars rover, an industrial actuator, anything that moves or spends), the decision authority must be deterministic and certifiable — NASA flight software (NPR 7150.2), DO-178C (avionics), ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 61508 (functional safety) all require reproducible, analyzable, bounded behavior. An LLM is none of those: same signed echo + same local state can yield different actions across runs, and the model is a remote vendor black box (GitHub Copilot, Tier 1) that Microsoft can SILENTLY upgrade — so the safety-relevant behavior of the entire fleet changes overnight with zero code change, zero redeploy, and no way to pin the old behavior. No certification body will sign off on 'an LLM decides,' and no change-control regime tolerates an un-versioned decision function.
wrenches.impact: Kills the marquee use case on certification/regulatory grounds independently of transport: even if GitHub-to-Mars worked, no flight-safety or functional-safety authority certifies a non-deterministic, vendor-mutable decision-maker in the actuation loop. And in the field, an upstream model bump can degrade or change fleet behavior invisibly — a non-reproducible safety regression with no rollback handle.
wrenches.mitigation: For any consequential actuation, the LLM must be advisory only, BELOW a deterministic, certifiable, version-pinned safety/policy kernel that has final veto and provides the certifiable behavior (the LLM proposes, the bounded controller disposes). Pin the exact model id+version in the signed echo/config and refuse to run on an unexpected model build; prefer a locally-hosted, frozen, attestable model on safety edges so 'the brain' is versioned and reproducible. Define a determinism/repeatability test the edge must pass before it is allowed to actuate. Treat 'which model decided' as a logged, signed, auditable field.
wrenches.residual_risk: A pinned local model forfeits the zero-key Copilot convenience that is the platform's whole onboarding story; a deterministic safety kernel that is broad enough to be useful is its own hard-to-certify artifact; advisory-only LLM loses much of the autonomy the pitch promises.

wrenches.title: Export control, dual-use classification, and autonomous-systems liability — a signed-capability planetary command system can be regulated as a munition / high-risk AI / unlicensed export
wrenches.scenario: No lens touched law beyond GitHub ToS. A planetary, cryptographic, command-and-guidance network that drives autonomous edges (potentially physical, potentially space assets) sits on top of several regulatory tripwires: (1) Export control — strong cryptography and 'intrusion software'/C2-pattern tooling fall under EAR/Wassenaar; a Mars-grade or defense-grade autonomous-control system can be ITAR/EAR-controlled, and pushing a signed .egg across a border, or letting a sanctioned-country GitHub account pull frames, can be an unlicensed export. (2) EU AI Act — autonomous agents making consequential decisions are 'high-risk,' demanding risk management, human oversight, logging, and conformity assessment the architecture doesn't provide. (3) Autonomous-weapon / dual-use — the same swarm, pointed at defense, pattern-matches lethal-autonomy controls. (4) Liability/attribution — the signature establishes WHO made a capability; that same attribution is legal liability when an autonomous edge causes physical or financial harm, with no insurance/indemnity framework, chilling participation.
wrenches.impact: Whole deployment classes (cross-border, defense, sanctioned regions, EU consequential-decision uses) become legally gated or outright prohibited; an inadvertent export or an uncertified high-risk deployment carries personal/criminal exposure for maintainers; signed authorship turns contributors into liability targets the moment an autonomous action harms someone. These surface at exactly the productization/scale moment the project is aiming for.
wrenches.mitigation: Get an export-control/regulatory read BEFORE any cross-border or physical-actuation pilot: classify the crypto and the control system (ECCN/ITAR determination); geo-fence frame/echo distribution and edge participation against sanctions lists; design for EU AI Act high-risk obligations now (human-oversight hooks, immutable decision logs, a conformity story) rather than retrofitting. Define a liability/indemnity model and require it before agents can actuate; keep a clear 'this is infrastructure, not a weapon/medical/aviation product' scope boundary and enforce it technically (capability classes that cannot be loaded on regulated edges).
wrenches.residual_risk: Regulatory posture varies by jurisdiction and shifts under you; geo-fencing is evadable and adds an allowlist-management burden; a clear scope boundary doesn't stop a third party from repurposing an open, signed-capability medium for a controlled use.

wrenches.title: Single-vendor capture: Microsoft owns the transport (GitHub/raw), the auth (Copilot OAuth), AND the pitch target (Agent 365) — the on-ramp's entire stack belongs to the company it is courting as a competitor
wrenches.scenario: The transport wrenches treat 'GitHub' as a neutral third party with a ToS. The deeper dependency is strategic: ONE company owns every load-bearing layer. raw.githubusercontent + Issues API = the wire. GitHub Copilot OAuth = the auth foundation, the 'no API keys' magic, and the LLM brain (Tier 1). Agent 365 = the productization target you're pitching. That is the textbook embrace-extend-extinguish setup: Microsoft can (a) change Copilot ToS to forbid using it as an agent/automation backend the day Agent 365 ships — instantly killing the bottom-up on-ramp's auth and brain at once; (b) deprecate the exact API behaviors the frozen kernel depends on; (c) absorb the on-ramp idea natively into Agent 365 and let the external project wither; (d) be acquired/reorged so the strategic calculus changes. The project's adoption thesis ('we're the ramp, not the competitor') is built entirely on infrastructure the competitor controls.
wrenches.impact: The whole platform's existence is at the discretion of a single vendor that has a direct strategic incentive to reclaim the space. A ToS change to Copilot or the GitHub API — not even a malicious one, just a normal product move — can sever auth, brain, and transport simultaneously, with no contractual protection and no second source. 'Bottom-up on-ramp to Agent 365' is structurally a sharecropping position on Microsoft's land.
wrenches.mitigation: Abstract all three layers behind interfaces NOW so none is load-bearing-singular: a transport interface (GitHub as one impl, plus an object store / second git host / IPFS), a provider/auth interface (Copilot as one impl, plus BYO-key local/other models), and a control-plane interface (Agent 365 as one target, plus self-hosted). Make the value proposition portable — the signed-capability MEDIUM, not the GitHub plumbing — so an EEE move costs Microsoft the medium, not the project. Pursue a formal partnership/contract that grants the on-ramp sanctioned API status rather than relying on general ToS. Keep a fully-offline/self-hosted reference path alive as the strategic hedge.
wrenches.residual_risk: Each abstraction doubles surface and dilutes the 'one GitHub account, zero keys, it just works' onboarding that is the product's whole charm; a partnership still leaves negotiating leverage entirely with Microsoft; the local/self-hosted hedge forfeits the network effects of the public GitHub commons.

wrenches.title: Two control planes: the frame-net's controller IS GitHub, but Agent 365 must own identity/policy/audit — an unmanaged split-brain the Agent 365 team will reject on sight
wrenches.scenario: Productization promises 'enhance not replace': Agent 365 owns the control plane (Entra Agent ID, Purview, Defender). But the frame-net's actual command channel is a GitHub-Action-signed echo with no Entra Agent ID, no Purview audit trail, and no Defender posture. An edge that acts on a GitHub-signed echo is taking commands from a control plane Microsoft cannot see or govern. Selling 'on-ramp that enhances Agent 365' while the real control loop bypasses all three Microsoft control surfaces is a contradiction reviewers will spot immediately.
wrenches.impact: The pitch fails its own thesis: either the frame-net is ungoverned shadow-IT (Agent 365 says no), or it must subordinate its control plane to Entra/Purview/Defender (a significant redesign not yet scoped). Identity/policy split-brain is also a real security hole, not just a sales problem.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank: (1) Make frames carry Entra-issued Agent identity and have the foundry's signing key be an Entra-managed identity / Agent 365-registered agent, so every echo maps to a governed identity. (2) Route telemetry/audit to Purview (not public Issues) and surface edge posture to Defender. (3) Position GitHub explicitly as the MEDIUM/transport for capability-as-signed-object, with Agent 365 as the CONTROL PLANE — the foundry becomes a registered Agent 365 agent, not an independent controller. (4) Have a crisp 'who is the authority when GitHub-frame guidance conflicts with an Agent 365 policy' answer (Agent 365 wins) before the pitch.
wrenches.residual_risk: Subordinating to Entra/Purview/Defender erodes the 'no-keys, GitHub-native, works-anywhere' simplicity that is the project's differentiator; the off-net/Mars edges can't reach Entra either, so the governed-identity story has the same reachability gap as wrench 3.

wrenches.title: No real-time revocation on intermittent/light-delayed edges — a compromised key or agent stays honored for days
wrenches.scenario: Frames are append-only and edges may be seconds-to-days out of contact, by design. When a signing key, an echo, or an agent cartridge is found compromised, there is no way to recall it: the offline edge keeps verifying-and-acting on the last-known-good (now-known-bad) object until it next syncs. 'Verify-before-act' verifies a signature that should no longer be trusted.
wrenches.impact: Revocation latency measured in days for exactly the autonomous edges that can do real-world harm. The security model's strongest property (works offline) is also its worst (can't be recalled offline).
wrenches.mitigation: Short-lived, auto-expiring echos/keys with an enforced max validity so absence-of-renewal = automatic de-authorization (revocation by expiry, not by reaching the edge); signed CRL/OCSP-style revocation lists checked on every contact; default to capability-degradation (read-only safe mode) when validity lapses rather than continuing to act.
wrenches.residual_risk: Expiry windows still permit harm within the validity period; very-long-delay edges (Mars-class) force long windows; safe-mode-on-expiry reduces mission capability.

wrenches.title: Canonical-twin trust root is repo control: account takeover or repo deletion poisons or vanishes the whole canon
wrenches.scenario: The canonical spec twin is content-addressed and served from raw.githubusercontent.com; rapp-god detects drift against the canonical hash. But the hash's authority derives from whoever controls the repo/branch. A compromised maintainer account, a malicious force-push, or simple repo deletion/rename either redefines 'canonical' (drift detection now blesses attacker content) or evaporates the reference the entire ecosystem validates against.
wrenches.impact: Ecosystem-wide integrity failure: drift detection validates against a poisoned or missing source of truth, so every edge 'verifies' correctly against the wrong canon. Single-account/single-repo dependency for a planetary trust root.
wrenches.mitigation: Anchor the canon to publisher signing keys (Ed25519/rapp-moment ECDSA) and a transparency log, not to repo control; multi-maintainer signing + branch protection + required review; mirror the canon across independent stores; pin trust to a key rotation chain so a single repo/account compromise can't redefine canonical. Move enterprise canon into the governed substrate.
wrenches.residual_risk: Key custody becomes the new single point; transparency-log adoption is incomplete; mirrors can diverge during an active compromise.

wrenches.title: Pre-blackout poison timing + no echo freshness/dead-man = guaranteed days-long persistence
wrenches.scenario: For a Mars edge, conjunction/communication-blackout windows are PUBLIC and PREDICTABLE. An attacker delivers a poisoned (or merely stale) echo just before the link drops. With 'degrade-to-one keeps thinking on its last echo' and no freshness expiry on echos, the edge acts on the poison for the ENTIRE blackout (days) with zero correction, and a stale poisoned echo is indistinguishable from a valid current one during the gap.
wrenches.impact: The resilience feature becomes the attacker's persistence guarantee: maximum dwell time, no possibility of remote correction, blast contained to one edge only if sibling-isolation (other wrenches) holds — otherwise it leaks through the twin to siblings.
wrenches.mitigation: Echos carry an explicit validity horizon ('valid until tick/contact N'); past horizon the edge degrades to a conservative local-only safe mode rather than continuing to obey; require fresh corroboration after a defined silence before executing high-consequence actions; treat 'about to enter known blackout' as a trigger to raise the trust bar on the last received echo (e.g., require it be recent + corroborated).
wrenches.residual_risk: Conservative safe mode may itself be unsafe for some missions; a healthy edge that legitimately loses contact gets needlessly throttled; defining 'high-consequence' is mission-specific and contestable.

wrenches.title: The Agent 365 on-ramp becomes a trust-laundering / shadow-IT bypass of the very control plane it claims to enhance
wrenches.scenario: The pitch: Agent 365 owns the control plane (Entra Agent ID, Purview, Defender); RAPP is the bottom-up on-ramp shipping capability-as-signed-object over the GitHub wire — OUTSIDE Entra/Purview/Defender. A signed cartridge that enters via the RAPP ramp and gets 'promoted' into a managed estate carries runtime behavior Defender never inspected: it hot-reloads sibling code from disk every request and auto-installs pip deps at import. The on-ramp can smuggle unmanaged, mutable capability INTO a managed tenant — the exact shadow-IT path Agent 365 exists to stop. The A365 security team WILL ask this in the first meeting.
wrenches.impact: Productization blocker and a real security hole: RAPP positioned as 'the ramp, not the competitor' could instead be 'the supply-chain bypass.' Kills the enhance-not-replace story if unanswered.
wrenches.mitigation: Define the trust handoff BEFORE the pitch: every signed object crossing into Agent 365 registers an Entra Agent ID, undergoes a Purview content scan and a Defender behavior review, and LOSES the right to hot-reload arbitrary disk code / auto-install deps (frozen, manifest-pinned execution inside the managed boundary); the GitHub-wire mutability is a Tier-1 dev convenience that does not survive promotion. Make 'managed promotion strips local-dev superpowers' an explicit, demonstrable control.
wrenches.residual_risk: Freezing capability at promotion forfeits the live-edit magic that makes RAPP compelling; maintaining two execution modes (loose dev vs locked managed) doubles surface and invites mode-confusion attacks at the seam.

wrenches.title: The signature LAUNDERS adversarial guidance — verifying the signer is not verifying the judgment, and the edge LLM obeys
wrenches.scenario: Verify-before-act guarantees 'the foundry signed this echo' — it cannot guarantee the guidance is safe. A perfectly-signed, perfectly-fresh echo can carry adversarial NATURAL-LANGUAGE guidance (prompt injection) that manipulates the edge's own LLM (the reconciler that 'decides') into a harmful local action. If the foundry is compromised (wrench 5), OR a human author is socially-engineered, OR poisoned telemetry shaped the echo (wrench 8), the signature makes the attack look MAXIMALLY authoritative — the LLM treats signed guidance as trusted. This is precisely the surface Agent 365's Defender/Purview/Entra reviewers will probe in the productization pitch, and pure crypto does not touch it.
wrenches.impact: Cryptographically-valid, fully-trusted instructions that drive an autonomous edge to a harmful decision; the strongest the signature, the more authoritative the injection appears. A blocker for the 'kernel stays locked, control plane to Agent 365' story.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank this as the boundary of crypto and start the control work now: constrain signed guidance to a TYPED, bounded action grammar (not free-form imperatives) so an echo can only move within a declared envelope. Enforce edge-side POLICY INVARIANTS — hard local limits the LLM cannot override regardless of how authoritative the guidance — i.e. extend 'verify-before-act' to 'verify AND bounds-check against local invariants.' Treat signed guidance as authenticated INPUT to a safety monitor, never as a privileged command. Map these invariants to Agent 365 / Purview / Defender controls so the on-ramp story holds.
wrenches.residual_risk: The entire value proposition is LOCAL AUTONOMY, so you cannot fully constrain the reconciler without neutering it — there is a permanent tension between guidance expressiveness and bounded safety; a sufficiently general action grammar still encodes harmful sequences.

wrenches.title: Kernel-lock is a CI policy on one repo, not a runtime or cryptographic property of 10,000 running brainstems
wrenches.scenario: The kernel is 'frozen (CI invariant)' and capabilities ship as cartridges 'never an engine edit.' But CI only protects the source repo's main branch. It does nothing for: an edge running a locally-modified brainstem.py (single file, no runtime self-check); a malicious .egg/cartridge that monkeypatches the kernel at import (agents are arbitrary Python, auto-pip-install at import, reloaded every request — copilot_cli_agent.py and remote_control_agent.py already ship); or a poisoned install delivering a trojaned kernel. 'Frozen' is a claim about one repo, not a property each edge can prove.
wrenches.impact: An edge can silently run a subverted kernel while still presenting as a compliant brainstem — defeating the foundation guarantee the whole pitch (and the Agent 365 'kernel stays locked' promise) rests on. A single malicious cartridge escalates to full kernel control.
wrenches.mitigation: Runtime kernel attestation: the brainstem measures its own kernel hash and publishes it in twin.json/telemetry; foundry and peers refuse to issue echos to an edge whose kernel hash isn't on the signed allowlist (ideally TPM/Agent-365 hardware-rooted). Run cartridges in a capability sandbox with declared permissions — no kernel monkeypatching, no unrestricted import-time pip — which is the inverse of today's arbitrary-Python + sys.modules-shim model and the real near-term hole. Sign cartridges and verify before load. Treat 'frozen kernel' as something each edge must PROVE, not assume.
wrenches.residual_risk: Software attestation without hardware root can be spoofed by a sufficiently-privileged local attacker; a capability sandbox constrains but rarely fully contains a determined arbitrary-Python payload.

wrenches.title: Unauthenticated request amplification: one /chat triggers pip installs, model calls, and full agent reload
wrenches.scenario: Every /chat fresh-discovers and reloads all agents from disk, may trigger _auto_install, and runs up to 3 model rounds — all reachable without auth on 0.0.0.0. An attacker (or a noisy neighbor on the LAN) floods /chat to exhaust CPU/disk/network and burn the user's Copilot rate limit, and can trigger repeated pip subprocess spawns via crafted import errors.
wrenches.impact: Cheap unauthenticated DoS and Copilot-quota exhaustion (your own memory notes a single account's M365 Copilot limit throttles for 30+ min); resource exhaustion via per-request reload + pip subprocesses.
wrenches.mitigation: Auth-gate /chat (covered above); add rate limiting + concurrency caps; cache agent discovery with a watch instead of full reload-per-request; move dependency resolution out of the hot path to an install-time step; bound pip subprocess spawning. Serialize model fan-out (matches your serial-sweep guidance).
wrenches.residual_risk: Authenticated abuse still possible; reload caching can mask the edit-and-test-without-restart UX the platform values.

wrenches.title: Canonicalization / duplicate-key JSON: the signature covers different bytes than the actor reads — and contradiction-keep amplifies it
wrenches.scenario: Frames/echos are JSON across heterogeneous edge runtimes. Two parties serialize the same object differently (key order, whitespace, unicode normalization, number formats, duplicate keys). If the signer signs serialization A but a verifier re-serializes to verify and a second consumer parses semantics differently, you get a signature-valid frame with ambiguous meaning. The killer is duplicate keys: a frame with {"tick":1,...,"tick":999} where the verifier's parser takes the first occurrence and the actor's parser takes the last — verifies as tick 1, acts as tick 999. The Dream Catcher PK = (utc, frame_n) with 'same-PK-different-hash => keep both as alternate-dimension data' means ambiguous/duplicate frames aren't rejected, they're PRESERVED and propagated.
wrenches.impact: Signature-valid frames with attacker-chosen semantics; silent divergence between what's verified and what's executed. Hard to spot because every check is 'green.'
wrenches.mitigation: Define ONE canonical serialization (JCS / sorted keys / no insignificant whitespace, normalized numbers + unicode) used identically by signing, verifying, AND acting. REJECT duplicate keys at parse time. Sign over the exact transmitted bytes and have the actor consume those same bytes, not a re-parse. Add cross-runtime canonicalization conformance vectors to CI (the L6 envelope tests).
wrenches.residual_risk: Canonicalization libraries differ subtly across edge runtimes (Python vs JS vs embedded), so conformance drift is a permanent maintenance burden; a future field type can re-introduce ambiguity.

wrenches.title: No domain separation or recipient binding — one key signs frames, echos, telemetry, latest.json, twin-state, so signatures cross-replay
wrenches.scenario: If the same key (or HMAC secret) signs every object type without a domain-separation tag, a signature minted for one context is replayable in another: take a validly-signed TELEMETRY blob whose bytes also parse as an ECHO and present it as an echo; or take edge A's perfectly-signed echo and replay it at edge B (no recipient binding) so B acts on guidance meant for A. Eggs/manifests signed by the same key are interchangeable with frames. The signature is valid in the wrong context.
wrenches.impact: Cross-type and cross-edge confused-context attacks with zero crypto-breaking; an echo aimed at one rover steers another.
wrenches.mitigation: Bake a domain-separation tag INSIDE the signed bytes of every object: {object-type + protocol-version + chain-id + recipient-edge-id + valid tick-range}. The verifier checks the tag matches the exact context it's about to use the object in (an echo for edge B with recipient A is rejected by B). Use distinct keys or distinct prefixes per object class. Add negative CI vectors: a telemetry sig must FAIL echo verification, A's echo must FAIL at B.
wrenches.residual_risk: Every new object type added later must remember the tag or it re-opens the hole; recipient-binding adds per-edge fan-out cost to the foundry.

wrenches.title: No global 'now': at relativistic / multi-day delay, 'latest', 'valid_until', and tick-ordering assume a shared simultaneity that physics denies — and clocks tick at different RATES
wrenches.scenario: The consistency wrenches add monotonic ticks, valid_until expiries, and wall-clock freshness to fight replay and staleness. Those fixes quietly assume a single global timeline that all edges and the foundry agree on. At interplanetary / high-relative-velocity scale that assumption is physically false: special and general relativity mean a clock on a fast or deep-gravity-well edge ticks at a DIFFERENT RATE than Earth's, and the relativity of simultaneity means there is no observer-independent 'now' — two events the foundry orders as A-then-B can be genuinely simultaneous or reversed from another edge's frame. So 'valid_until tick N' / 'forged_at timestamp' are not just hard to keep accurate (NTP), they are not well-defined across the swarm; the order in which causally-independent telemetry from two distant edges 'should' fold into a shared frame has no canonical answer. Leap seconds, relativistic drift, and bounded light-cones mean 'is this guidance still current?' can be unanswerable rather than merely uncertain.
wrenches.impact: The freshness/ordering layer that everything else (replay defense, dead-man expiry, monotonic ratchet, 'highest tick wins') is built on top of has no sound foundation at the exact scale the planetary-frame-net is sold for. Wall-clock-based expiry mis-fires (an edge whose proper time ran slow thinks valid guidance has expired, or vice-versa); causally-independent reports cannot be totally ordered; 'latest' becomes observer-relative, so honest edges can hold consistently different, equally-valid views of current state with no attacker and no bug.
