wrenches.mitigation: Stop relying on wall-clock time for security/correctness; order by CAUSALITY, not time — use logical clocks / vector clocks / a hash-DAG (prev-hash already gives partial order) so 'after' means 'causally after,' which IS frame-invariant, rather than 'later on a shared clock,' which isn't. Express echo validity in terms the edge can evaluate locally and causally (ticks-since-my-last-contact, contact-window counts, corroboration counts) rather than absolute timestamps. Accept and design for eventual, causal consistency — never strong/linearizable 'current state' — across high-delay edges; make every action idempotent and commutative where possible so divergent-then-reconciled orderings still converge safely. Account for relativistic clock drift explicitly in any timing budget for true space edges.
wrenches.residual_risk: Causal/logical ordering leaves genuinely-concurrent events unordered, so a tie-break policy is still needed and can be gamed or simply produce divergence; vector-clock state grows with the swarm; and some real missions need a wall-clock deadline that no logical clock can express.

wrenches.title: A 'planetary' nervous system on one US vendor's free tier is a single-vendor, single-jurisdiction SPOF for the whole organism
wrenches.scenario: The entire transport, identity store, compute (Actions), and trust anchor are github.com — one US company, much of it on free tiers. GitHub is blocked or degraded in several countries, subject to US export-control and sanctions, can suspend any account for ToS at will, and has its own outages (the whole fleet's read/write/compute share GitHub's incident timeline). A literal 'brainstem on Mars' whose lifeline is github.com inherits every GitHub outage, policy change, and geopolitical action as a fleet-wide event.
wrenches.impact: Strategic concentration risk: a GitHub outage, policy shift, sanctions action, or business decision can degrade or kill the entire planetary swarm at once, with no second source. For a system pitched as resilient/degrade-to-one, the transport itself is a global SPOF.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank transport abstraction: define a transport interface (read frames / write telemetry / store twin) with GitHub as one implementation, and prototype at least one alternative (S3+CDN, IPFS, a self-hosted git, or the customer's own Azure storage for the Agent 365 path) so the swarm can fail over or run dual-homed. For the Agent 365 productization, make the customer's own Microsoft cloud the primary transport, reducing the GitHub dependency to the public/community on-ramp.
wrenches.residual_risk: A transport abstraction adds surface and a multi-homed swarm risks split-brain across transports; full vendor independence is expensive and erodes the 'just GitHub, no server' simplicity that makes the system adoptable.

wrenches.title: Issues are MUTABLE — 'append-only ledger' is a convention GitHub does not enforce; history can be edited/deleted, rewriting the record
wrenches.scenario: GitHub Issues and comments can be edited and deleted by the author or any repo admin; contents files can be force-pushed/rewritten. The ledger's 'append-only' and 'immutable history' properties are social conventions, not cryptographic or platform guarantees. A panicked operator, a compromised admin, or an attacker with write access can silently rewrite or erase telemetry/frames, and the prev-hash chain only protects forward integrity if every reader has independently pinned earlier hashes.
wrenches.impact: Loss of auditability and non-repudiation: the record an investigator or the foundry relies on can be altered after the fact with no tamper-evidence at the platform layer. Undermines the whole 'verifiable signed chain' value proposition.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank: (1) Make append-only cryptographic, not platform-trusted: every reader (and the foundry) pins and persists prev-hashes locally; a chain that doesn't extend a pinned hash is rejected as a rewrite. (2) Periodic external anchoring of the chain head (e.g., publish the head hash to a transparency log / a second origin / a timestamping authority) so deletions/rewrites are detectable. (3) Restrict write access narrowly (GitHub App, branch protection, no admin-by-default) and treat any history divergence as an incident. (4) Sign every appended record so an edit invalidates its signature.
wrenches.residual_risk: You can DETECT tampering after anchoring, but you cannot PREVENT a GitHub-side deletion; recovery depends on every party having pinned/replicated the segments, which light/offline edges may not have.

wrenches.title: Twin inbox poisoning: 'reach the edge through its twin' makes the inbox/ a writable attack surface for adversarial guidance crafted to survive local LLM reconciliation
wrenches.scenario: Others reach an edge through its public twin (twin.json + inbox/ of echos+messages). Whoever can write to inbox/ — via PR (a compromised maintainer or an auto-merge bot), the API, or a repo-write compromise — can drop messages the edge will read and feed into its local-LLM reconciliation. A motivated attacker doesn't need to forge a SIGNED frame; they craft unsigned 'messages' or social-engineering content shaped to bias the edge's own LLM into a harmful local decision (prompt-injection of the reconciler).
wrenches.impact: Adversary influences edge behavior WITHOUT holding the signing key, by exploiting the reconciliation step rather than the signature. Scales to any edge whose inbox is writable, and is hard to detect because the edge 'decided for itself'.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank: (1) Strict trust separation in the reconciler: SIGNED echos are guidance; unsigned inbox messages are UNTRUSTED DATA and must never be treated as instructions (treat as content to summarize/report, not commands to follow). (2) Lock down inbox writes — signed messages only, sender allowlist, no anonymous PR auto-merge into inbox paths. (3) Prompt-injection hardening of the reconciler: structured guidance schema (not free text), provenance tags on every input, and a policy that local actions require a signed echo, not an inbox message. (4) Rate-limit + quarantine new inbox senders.
wrenches.residual_risk: Local-LLM reconciliation is inherently susceptible to cleverly crafted inputs; even with signed-only guidance, a compromised legitimate sender can still inject, and 'untrusted-data-not-instructions' is a discipline that LLMs imperfectly honor.

wrenches.title: latest.json update is an unlocked lost-update/split-brain race in the foundry
wrenches.scenario: latest.json is the cheap heartbeat pointer. If two foundry runs overlap (scheduled + manual trigger, a retry, or a failed-over standby), or two writers touch latest.json, git push contention causes one to fail or force-overwrite, producing a lost update or split-brain on the head pointer. There is no locking on the 'current frame' designator, and CDN staleness (wrench 1) compounds it so different readers resolve different heads.
wrenches.impact: Readers disagree on what 'latest' is, or a tick is silently dropped/overwritten; the heartbeat — the cheapest, most-relied-on signal — becomes unreliable exactly when the foundry is under stress or failing over.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank: (1) Single-writer discipline: leader election / a workflow concurrency group so only one foundry run can publish at a time (concurrency: cancel-in-progress or queue). (2) CAS-style updates: write the new frame content-addressed first, then atomically swap latest.json only if its current hash matches the expected prev (compare-and-swap via the contents API's expected-sha). (3) Idempotent forging with a persisted cursor so a retried/overlapping run cannot double-advance. (4) Readers verify latest.json's referenced frame actually exists+verifies before trusting the pointer.
wrenches.residual_risk: CAS via the contents API still races against CDN read-staleness on the verify side; leader election adds its own failure modes, and a force-push by an admin bypasses all of it.

wrenches.title: Secrets-in-agents and no Key Vault path: developers embed credentials in plaintext .py shipped to RAR
wrenches.scenario: Agents are plaintext Python files on disk and in the community registry. With no secret-management integration, developers inevitably hardcode API keys/connection strings in perform(), then publish the agent to RAR or share it as an .egg — leaking secrets into a public registry and into every machine that installs it.
wrenches.impact: Credential leakage at registry scale; one popular community agent with an embedded key burns that key everywhere. Standard secret-scanning would light up the registry.
wrenches.mitigation: Provide a first-class secret-reference mechanism (Key Vault / OS keychain / env injection) so agents reference rather than embed secrets; mandatory secret-scanning gate on RAR publish that blocks on detection; document and lint against inline credentials; auto-revoke flow on detected leak.
wrenches.residual_risk: Scanners miss obfuscated/encoded secrets; a determined author can still smuggle credentials; revocation depends on the issuer's API.

wrenches.title: 'Append-only telemetry via the Issues API' isn't actually append-only or immutable — and the write token is an impersonation oracle
wrenches.scenario: GitHub Issues can be EDITED, DELETED, hidden, locked, and rate-limited; 'append-only' is the builders' discipline, not a property GitHub enforces. An attacker with issue-edit rights (or who wins a race, or who leaks the write token) MUTATES or DELETES the telemetry the foundry reads, reorders history, or floods issues to exhaust the foundry's read rate-limit (a denial-of-foundry that starves all edges of fresh echos). A leaked scoped write token lets an attacker post telemetry as any edge (feeding wrench 8).
wrenches.impact: Tamperable/ deletable 'source of truth' for echo forging; DoS of the foundry; cross-edge telemetry impersonation. Undermines the audit trail the whole scheme leans on.
wrenches.mitigation: Make telemetry self-verifying independent of GitHub's mutability: each entry edge-signed AND prev-hash-chained, so any edit/delete/reorder shows up as a broken chain that the foundry detects and refuses to ingest past. Foundry publishes a signed attestation of 'exactly what I ingested at tick N.' Least-privilege, per-edge, short-lived write tokens. Treat issue edit/delete events as detectable tamper alarms, not silent.
wrenches.residual_risk: Deletion still causes irrecoverable availability loss (you detect the gap, the data is gone); GitHub remains a trusted third party for availability and rate-limit, outside your control; flooding/DoS of the read path is hard to fully prevent.

wrenches.title: Unbounded append-only log: repo limits, forever-growing cold-start, and no checkpointing
wrenches.scenario: Frames + echos + telemetry issues grow forever by design. GitHub recommends repos under ~1GB and hard-caps raw files at 100MB; Issues accumulate without clean deletion; API pagination cost explodes; raw-CDN of large files slows. Critically, a NEW edge bootstrapping must walk prev-hash from genesis to verify the chain — cold-start cost grows linearly with mission age, forever.
wrenches.impact: The transport silently degrades: cold-start eventually becomes impractical, the repo risks being flagged/archived, and heartbeat reads get slow. A 'forever' system with O(n) bootstrap is a time bomb.
wrenches.mitigation: Introduce signed CHECKPOINT frames (a periodic state-root that summarizes accumulated state, like a blockchain snapshot). Cold-start = fetch latest checkpoint, verify its signature, then verify FORWARD only — never from genesis. Prune/archive behind checkpoints. Rotate telemetry into closed/archived issues or a dedicated archive repo on a schedule; keep latest.json O(1). Shard content-addressed frames into hash-prefix directories to avoid mega-directories. Define explicit retention windows now, before volume forces it.
wrenches.residual_risk: Checkpoints become trust shortcuts — a forged/buggy checkpoint short-circuits forward verification, so checkpoint signing must be as protected as the foundry key; pruned history is unrecoverable for forensic replay.

wrenches.title: Heartbeat thundering herd: raw rate-limits cause correlated fleet blindness
wrenches.scenario: Every edge polls latest.json. raw.githubusercontent enforces rate limits and serves via CDN; at thousands of edges, or when many edges egress through a shared ground-station/relay IP, they collectively trip a shared rate limit -> 429 -> blind. Worst case is a synchronized poll storm right after a new frame publishes (everyone refreshes at once).
wrenches.impact: Correlated loss of read access — large fractions of the fleet go blind simultaneously, precisely when fresh guidance just dropped.
wrenches.mitigation: Jittered, exponentially-backed-off polling. Use conditional requests (ETag / If-None-Match) so unchanged latest.json rides the CDN cache nearly free. Co-located edges behind a shared link share ONE fetch via a local relay that caches and rebroadcasts — this fits the twin model (the twin/relay is the front door). Keep latest.json tiny; compare ticks locally to avoid refetching frame bodies you already have.
wrenches.residual_risk: A shared-egress relay becomes its own local SPOF and rate-limit aggregation point; conditional-request savings still leave a floor of requests that can saturate at extreme scale.

wrenches.title: Foundry non-determinism blocks failover and audit, and re-creates split-brain at the source
wrenches.scenario: If the foundry uses an LLM with temperature, reads wall-clock, or iterates telemetry in unspecified order, its echo output is non-reproducible. You then cannot (a) audit whether a given echo was poisoned vs legitimately derived, nor (b) run a hot/cold standby foundry that AGREES with the primary — two non-deterministic foundries diverge, manufacturing the exact split-brain the standby was meant to prevent.
wrenches.impact: No trustworthy failover (standby disagrees), no forensic replay after a suspected poisoning, and a second foundry becomes a second conflicting writer.
wrenches.mitigation: Make the foundry a PURE, DETERMINISTIC function of (ordered+deduped telemetry set, frame chain): fixed iteration order, no wall-clock in the consistency-critical path, LLM steps pinned/seeded or kept entirely OUT of the deterministic core (LLM proposes, deterministic logic disposes). Sign the hash of the exact input-set into each output frame so any party can re-derive and verify the echo, and a standby provably re-produces identical output.
wrenches.residual_risk: Pinned LLMs still drift across provider model updates/deprecations, so the deterministic guarantee depends on freezing model versions; fully removing the LLM from the critical path may reduce echo quality, a quality-vs-verifiability tradeoff.

wrenches.title: The always-public twin is a reconnaissance, targeting, and privacy goldmine even with perfect crypto
wrenches.scenario: Every edge has an always-public front door exposing identity, last-known state, position, health, contact times, and a telemetry outbox — on public GitHub. Even with flawless signatures this LEAKS: where every edge is, when it last checked in (predict contact windows), which node is weak/lonely (target it), and the peer graph. For a contested edge ('brainstem on Mars', a drone) this is physical-targeting intelligence; for terrestrial enterprise edges (employee laptops) it is public location/behavior telemetry — a privacy and regulatory problem. The adversary breaks nothing; you published the map.
wrenches.impact: Pattern-of-life and physical/operational targeting of high-value edges; GDPR/Purview/compliance exposure for enterprise deployments. Metadata leakage that crypto cannot fix because confidentiality was never a goal of the design.
wrenches.mitigation: Minimize and compartmentalize public twin data: publish only what rendezvous strictly requires (identity + verify key + an opaque pointer); keep state/telemetry in an access-controlled, encrypted channel readable only by authorized peers. Coarsen, delay, and jitter position and contact-time data to defeat pattern-of-life. Make 'public twin' a per-field consent decision defaulting to minimal, not all-or-nothing. Route terrestrial/enterprise twins through the Agent 365 control plane (Purview-governed) instead of raw public GitHub.
wrenches.residual_risk: Some metadata (existence, rough cadence) must remain public for discovery; encryption shifts risk onto reader-key management and still leaks traffic-volume patterns.

wrenches.title: No root-of-trust ceremony or succession plan — the meta-governance gap under every other control
wrenches.scenario: Even with quorum + asymmetric crypto, the unstated question is: where does the ROOT of trust come from, who holds it, and what happens when the founder/maintainer is gone, coerced, or the root key is lost? Failure modes: a single founder holds the de-facto root (bus factor / coercion / the $5-wrench attack); no succession means root loss bricks canon and authority rotation forever; and there is no way to rotate the root without trusting the (possibly already-compromised) old root. A planetary system inherits whatever this one person's GitHub account inherits.
wrenches.impact: The entire trust hierarchy terminates at an undocumented, possibly-personal, single point of human failure — coercion, death, or key loss either captures or permanently bricks the system's ability to update canon, evict, or rotate authority.
wrenches.mitigation: Define a documented, ceremonial root-of-trust (this ecosystem already has a CONSTITUTION culture to anchor it to): threshold-held root key (Shamir / m-of-n across independent, multi-jurisdiction custodians), a published succession and rotation procedure, and swarm ratification of any root rotation via the transparency log + quorum, with anti-coercion measures (time-locks, geographic separation). For the enterprise pitch, anchor to Agent 365's Entra root so the customer's existing identity authority — not a personal GitHub account — is the trust anchor.
wrenches.residual_risk: Threshold custody is operationally heavy and rarely exercised, so the recovery path may be untested when actually needed; collusion or simultaneous coercion of a custodian quorum still captures the root.

wrenches.title: Emergent multi-agent dynamics: delayed-feedback control-loop instability, synchronized thundering-herds, tragedy-of-the-commons on the shared rate budget, and self-replicating-egg runaway
wrenches.scenario: No lens treated the swarm as a DYNAMICAL SYSTEM, only as a set of nodes under attack. The loop telemetry -> foundry -> echo -> edge-behavior -> new-telemetry is a closed feedback control loop with large, variable transport delay (CDN TTL + Action jitter + light-delay). Classic control theory says feedback with delay and gain oscillates: edges over-correct against stale guidance, the foundry over-corrects against the resulting telemetry, and the fleet resonates or limit-cycles — with NO attacker. Adjacent emergent failures: (1) Synchronized thundering-herd — all edges read the same fresh echo at the same TTL-boundary and act/poll simultaneously, creating correlated load spikes and self-inflicted DoS. (2) Tragedy-of-the-commons — each edge rationally writes more telemetry for better echos; collectively they exhaust the shared single-account rate budget and trip the ban (a coordination failure, not a flood attack). (3) Runaway replication — eggs are self-bootstrapping and agents can install agents; a benign-but-buggy 'spread this capability' loop becomes grey-goo across the fleet. (4) Heterogeneous-model divergence — edges on different silently-updated model builds reconcile the same echo into DIFFERENT behaviors, fragmenting the swarm's effective policy.
wrenches.impact: Whole-fleet misbehavior with no malice and no single faulty node: oscillation that looks like an attack but is the architecture resonating with itself; correlated load that self-DoSes the transport; a rational-actor collapse of the shared write budget; and unbounded self-replication that the kernel's open agent-loading enables. These are the failure modes that only appear at scale, exactly where the pitch wants to be, and they're invisible in single-node testing.
wrenches.mitigation: Treat the swarm as a control system: add per-edge jitter/randomized cadence (no TTL-synchronized herds), damping/hysteresis on how fast an edge responds to guidance changes, and rate-of-change limits so the loop can't resonate. Model loop stability explicitly (gain x delay margins) before scaling. Enforce a global, fair-share telemetry budget with backpressure so no edge's local optimum starves the commons. Cap replication: eggs/agents that install other agents require explicit authorization and a generation/depth limit; alarm on replication fan-out. Pin model versions per cohort so reconciliation is homogeneous. Run chaos/scale simulations (hundreds-to-thousands of edges) to surface emergent modes before a real fleet does.
wrenches.residual_risk: Damping that prevents oscillation also slows legitimate fast response; fair-share budgeting needs a coordinator that is itself a SPOF; replication caps can block a legitimate mass-update; emergent dynamics are by nature hard to fully predict from component testing.

wrenches.title: Physical edge reality: cosmic-ray bit-flips and fault-injection make crypto checks fail-OPEN with no attacker, and physical capture of an edge extracts its private key (no edge HSM)
wrenches.scenario: All crypto/identity wrenches assume a LOGICAL adversary on the wire. The physical edge defeats them for free. (1) Single-event upsets — a Mars/space or high-altitude edge is bombarded by radiation; a cosmic ray flips a bit in the byte that holds the result of a signature comparison, in the loaded public key, or in the persisted monotonic high-water-mark tick. A flipped 'verify failed' becomes 'verify passed' (fail-open), or a corrupted key silently rejects all legitimate frames (fail-closed brick), or a flipped tick resets the replay ratchet — none of which any signature scheme detects, because the corruption is downstream of the math. (2) Fault injection / Rowhammer — a locally-present attacker glitches power/clock to skip the verify branch. (3) Physical capture — a deployed IoT body, drone, or rover is stolen/captured; with no HSM/secure-element on a cheap edge (the foundry got an HSM in the mitigations; the edges did not), the attacker extracts the private signing key from flash/RAM and can now mint valid telemetry AND read every sibling echo addressed through twins, indistinguishably from the real edge — and revocation can't reach offline siblings for a contact window.
wrenches.impact: Cryptographic guarantees evaporate at the hardware boundary: a random cosmic ray can make an offline edge accept a forgery or brick itself, and capturing one cheap physical body yields a swarm-valid identity that passes every verify-before-act check. The architecture's trust language ('signed = trusted') has no answer to 'the bit that said verified got flipped' or 'the attacker is holding the device.'
wrenches.mitigation: Defend the silicon: ECC memory + radiation-hardened or redundant compute on space/high-radiation edges; verify-twice / triple-redundant comparison with voting for security-critical branches so a single flip can't flip the verdict; checksum/duplicate the loaded public key and the persisted tick and re-validate before each use; fail-CLOSED by default on any integrity mismatch. Put the private key in a tamper-responsive secure element/TPM on edges (not just the foundry) so capture doesn't yield the raw key; design tamper-detection that zeroizes the key and triggers an out-of-band revocation. Assume some edges WILL be captured: per-edge keys (already needed), fast quarantine of a captured edge's twin, and limiting what any single edge key can authorize.
wrenches.residual_risk: Rad-hard/secure-element hardware contradicts the 'cheap, commodity, runs-anywhere brainstem' premise and raises BOM cost; redundant verification adds latency/power on constrained edges; a captured edge's key is still golden until revocation propagates across light-delay, and a sufficiently resourced attacker defeats consumer-grade tamper response.

wrenches.title: The reconciling LLM is a remote, mutable, unowned brain — silent model swaps change decisions non-reproducibly, and the swarm's own public data trains its future models (a long-game self-poisoning feedback loop)
wrenches.scenario: Memory-poisoning and prompt-injection are covered, but those attack the CONTEXT. The model itself is the unaddressed soft underbelly. (1) Vendor mutability — the edge 'reconciles' via GitHub Copilot, a black box Microsoft updates silently; the same signed echo + same local state yields a DIFFERENT decision after an upgrade you didn't authorize, so the safety-relevant function is non-reproducible and un-pinnable across the fleet. (2) Training-data feedback loop — the architecture PUBLISHES frames, telemetry, and twin state to public GitHub, which is scraped into the next generation of training corpora (GHArchive -> datasets -> model training). An adversary who shapes the swarm's public frames over time is mounting a slow data-poisoning attack not on a prompt but on the future MODEL that will reconcile those very frames — the swarm poisons its own future brain, and so does anyone watching it. (3) Provider compulsion/injection — the same vendor that owns the brain can be compelled to, or accidentally, bias outputs. The decision authority is a borrowed, drifting, publicly-trainable function with no version handle.
wrenches.impact: Fleet behavior can change overnight from an upstream model bump with no code change and no rollback; long-lived public exposure turns the swarm into both a training signal and a poisoning surface for the models that will later run it, a feedback loop with a multi-month-to-year delay that is nearly impossible to attribute; and the most safety-critical function in the system is the one piece the operator neither owns nor versions.
wrenches.mitigation: Pin and attest the exact model build in the signed config; refuse to actuate on an unexpected model version; prefer locally-hosted, frozen, attestable models on consequential edges so the brain is owned and reproducible. Keep the swarm's operational data OUT of public, scrapable surfaces (private/encrypted telemetry, per the GDPR/exfil fixes) so it can't feed adversarial training. Periodically re-baseline behavior against a golden decision-test suite to detect silent model drift. Never treat a vendor LLM's judgment as authoritative for safety — gate it under a deterministic policy layer (ties to the certification wrench).
wrenches.residual_risk: Local frozen models cost the zero-setup Copilot onboarding and need their own update/patch lifecycle; behavioral re-baselining catches drift only after it ships; you cannot un-train data already harvested into deployed models.

wrenches.title: Append-only chained frames + GitHub repo soft-limits = an inevitable wall you cannot compact
wrenches.scenario: Frames are append-only and chained by prev-hash; telemetry is append-only into issues/commits. GitHub soft-limits repos around 5GB (warnings, degraded git performance well before), and git operations slow as history and object count grow. An immutable, prev-hash-chained, never-pruned structure cannot be compacted without breaking the chain that the whole verify-before-act model depends on. So the design guarantees the transport repos grow without bound until they hit GitHub's limits and degrade.
wrenches.impact: Over months/years the frame and telemetry repos slow, then warn, then degrade clones/reads for every edge — a slow-motion fleet-wide performance and availability decay baked into the data model.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank a checkpoint-and-archive design from day one: periodic signed 'checkpoint' frames that summarize and allow safe pruning of pre-checkpoint history (the chain re-anchors to the checkpoint hash, not genesis), shard transport repos by epoch/region so each stays small, and cold-archive old telemetry to object storage (or a fresh repo) with a pointer. Decide the retention/compaction policy before the chain grows.
wrenches.residual_risk: Checkpoint-pruning weakens the 'fully auditable from genesis' guarantee (you must trust the checkpoint signer for pre-checkpoint history), and offline edges that missed the checkpoint can't validate the re-anchored chain until they sync it.

wrenches.title: Per-twin PKI with no server and no CA: revocation is unsolved — you cannot push a CRL to an offline Mars edge
wrenches.scenario: Millions of public twins each get an Ed25519 identity (twin.json + verify key). That's a planetary PKI: key generation, distribution, rotation, AND revocation — with no server and no certificate authority. Revocation is the hard part: when a twin's key is compromised, there is no way to push a revocation to edges that are offline for hours-to-days (or to a Mars edge mid-transit). An edge acting on its last verified echo will keep trusting a revoked key until it next syncs, which may be after the attacker has used it.
wrenches.impact: Compromise recovery is slow and non-uniform across the fleet: a stolen twin key remains effective on every not-yet-synced edge, so the window of fleet-wide trust in a known-bad identity can be days — directly contradicting 'verify-before-act' guarantees during the most critical moment.
wrenches.mitigation: Bank a revocation strategy suited to disconnection: short-lived keys / signed validity windows so a key auto-expires without an explicit CRL (an unsynced edge stops trusting it on its own), gossiped revocation frames so any contact propagates the bad-key list, threshold/quorum identity so one stolen key isn't sufficient, and a documented out-of-band emergency re-pin. Lean on Agent 365's Entra Agent ID as the authority for the M365-connected subset.
wrenches.residual_risk: Short-lived keys raise re-issuance traffic (more quota) and can strand a long-disconnected edge whose key expired mid-flight, forcing a degrade-to-one with no trustable identity until re-contact.

wrenches.title: Public twins (twin.json/state.json/inbox/outbox) are a fleet reconnaissance and targeting map
wrenches.scenario: Every edge has an always-reachable public GitHub front door exposing identity, verify key, last-known state, and a mailbox. An adversary enumerates the org's repos and trivially builds a live map of every edge: where it is, what it can do, when it last checked in, and its current state — then times attacks to link windows and targets the weakest/most-stale nodes.
wrenches.impact: Free OSINT for an attacker; aids the echo-poisoning and Leviathan-RCE wrenches by revealing exactly which body to hit and when it's offline (and thus running on a stale, more-poisonable echo).
wrenches.mitigation: Minimize public twin contents to an opaque identifier + verify key + signed liveness pointer; move state/inbox/outbox behind authenticated, authz'd access on the governed substrate; rotate twin identifiers; don't leak capability lists or precise location/timing. Private-by-default twins for enterprise tenants.
wrenches.residual_risk: Any always-reachable front door leaks metadata (timing, existence); reachability-vs-privacy is an inherent tension in the twin design.

wrenches.title: Anti-rollback gap: latest.json + HMAC chain can be replayed/forked to pin an edge to stale or chosen guidance
wrenches.scenario: latest.json {tick,hash,updated} is the cheap heartbeat. With a shared HMAC secret and no monotonic freshness proof, an attacker (or a MITM on the read path, or a writer to the repo) replays an older signed latest.json/frame to roll an edge back to prior guidance, or forks the prev-hash chain to feed a chosen branch. Light-delay + offline-degrade means the edge can't cheaply cross-check freshness.
wrenches.impact: Downgrade/replay attack steering edges onto outdated or attacker-selected aim while every signature still verifies. Particularly damaging combined with degrade-to-one (edge commits to the stale echo for days).
wrenches.mitigation: Add monotonic, signed freshness: strictly increasing signed ticks with a max-age that flips the edge to safe-mode when exceeded; asymmetric signatures (not shared HMAC) so a reader-side compromise can't forge; include a signed chain-tip checkpoint and reject any tick <= last-seen. Pin trust to the publisher key, not the transport.
wrenches.residual_risk: Max-age safe-mode trades autonomy for safety; clock/skew handling across light-delay edges is hard; a key-compromised publisher can still issue fresh malicious frames.

wrenches.title: IP/licensing contamination: community agents and mixed licenses (PolyForm-NC) ingested into a commercial enterprise
wrenches.scenario: RAR community agents and .egg organisms carry unknown or restrictive provenance; parts of the ecosystem are PolyForm-Noncommercial (e.g., hologram). An enterprise auto-installing community capabilities can pull in NC-licensed or copyleft code and use it commercially in violation of license, or ingest code of unclear copyright/ownership into its environment.
wrenches.impact: Legal/IP exposure for the customer and for Microsoft as the distributor; open-source-program-office review will block ingestion of unvetted, mixed-license third-party code into an enterprise product surface.
wrenches.mitigation: Mandatory license metadata + SPDX on every registry object; license-policy gate that blocks NC/copyleft/unknown from enterprise install; provenance/ownership attestation per publisher; a curated, license-cleared enterprise catalog distinct from the open community registry; clear EULA on what 'install' grants.
wrenches.residual_risk: Declared licenses can be wrong/forged; transitive dependencies carry their own terms; ownership of community-authored code can be disputed.

wrenches.title: rapp-god is detect-not-prevent and is itself attackable via alert fatigue and baseline blinding
wrenches.scenario: rapp-god detects drift against canonical hashes. Two attacks: (1) Alert fatigue — flood the canonical surface with legitimate-looking near-miss edits so rapp-god fires constant drift alerts; responders mute it; the REAL malicious drift hides in the noise. (2) Baseline blinding — if the detector's own canonical baseline is poisoned (ties to the unsigned-canon wrench), it reports 'no drift' while everything is compromised. Nobody watches the watcher.
wrenches.impact: A security control that is either tuned-out or silently subverted gives false assurance — arguably worse than no detector because it manufactures confidence.
wrenches.mitigation: Verify against a SIGNED baseline, and alarm loudly on changes to the baseline itself (a baseline change is the highest-severity event, not a silent re-anchor); risk-rank drift so high-impact changes can't be buried under cosmetic noise; require human-acknowledged signed approval for any canon change; run an independent second watcher with a different trust root to cross-check rapp-god; transparency-log every detection and every baseline mutation.
wrenches.residual_risk: Two watchers with a shared root share a single compromise; risk-ranking is itself a heuristic an attacker tunes against; humans still rubber-stamp under volume.

wrenches.title: raw CDN cache fragmentation = infrastructure-induced split-brain across edges
wrenches.scenario: Different edges fetching the SAME frame URL can get DIFFERENT cached versions from different raw.githubusercontent CDN PoPs (cache fragmentation by geography/time). Two edges — one Earth-side, one relayed via a different region — can act on different 'latest' frames for minutes with NO attacker involved. An attacker who can influence cache keys (query strings, Vary headers) can deliberately PIN a target edge to a stale frame while others advance.
wrenches.impact: Consistency of the shared world-model is undermined by the transport itself; honest edges silently diverge (split-brain), and the divergence window is attacker-extendable into a targeted isolation.
wrenches.mitigation: Don't depend on CDN coherence for correctness: edges converge via the SIGNED chain (prev-hash + monotonic tick) and reconcile to the highest verifiable tick they can fetch from ANY mirror, not 'whatever this PoP served'; cross-check the heartbeat against multiple fetch paths; treat per-PoP staleness as expected and design idempotent, tick-ordered application so order, not arrival-copy, decides state.
wrenches.residual_risk: Multi-path fetching costs bandwidth on constrained links; an attacker controlling all reachable paths to one edge still isolates it; chain-convergence assumes edges can eventually reach a fresh copy at all.

wrenches.title: Forced-staleness / eclipse: cachedGhJson + tick-without-a-clock + a wipeable tick-floor turns availability denial into a rollback primitive
wrenches.scenario: An offline edge can't trust its own clock and has no online time source, so freshness must lean on logical ticks. A network adversary who can force 'fetch failure' (cachedGhJson then 'returns last-cached state') pins the edge on stale cache indefinitely — an eclipse — while it believes it's merely offline. Combined with fail-closed (wrench 4), the attacker keeps it on an increasingly-stale-but-verified echo with no alarm because 'no network' looks normal on this medium. And the rollback protection (highest-seen tick persisted to storage) is defeated by wiping/rolling back that storage on an unattended edge, after which old signed frames replay as 'new' again.
wrenches.impact: Indefinite operation on attacker-chosen stale guidance; defeats every freshness defense by attacking time/cache/durable-state rather than signatures. On an autonomous safety edge, 'frozen on old orders' can be as harmful as wrong orders.
wrenches.mitigation: Use a monotonic LOGICAL tick (not wall-clock) as the primary freshness primitive, persisted to tamper-resistant/append-only durable storage and NEVER decreased even across reboot/restore. Add a heartbeat-of-life EXPECTATION: if no fresh verified echo within N ticks, the edge transitions to a conservative safe posture and screams on every channel rather than silently coasting. Bound the staleness an edge will act on before requiring re-contact. Pair with operational-key EXPIRY so even a storage wipe can't resurrect long-dead frames.
wrenches.residual_risk: There is a real, irreducible tension: degrade-to-one EXISTS to keep acting on old guidance when isolated, so any staleness bound either cuts off a legitimately-isolated edge (availability) or admits some replay window (integrity). Storage tamper-resistance on cheap unattended edges is limited.

wrenches.title: Transport == one GitHub repo: suspension, rename, or going private kills the planetary wire
wrenches.scenario: The entire transport is one GitHub org/repo accessed anonymously via raw.githubusercontent.com and the Issues API. GitHub can suspend the account (ToS/abuse, plausibly triggered by the issue-spam vector in wrench #4), a rename breaks every raw URL, flipping the repo private kills anonymous raw reads, a DMCA/legal action, or org-level rate-limiting all sever the fleet from its only wire.
wrenches.impact: Correlated, total loss of transport for every edge at once — a business/governance SPOF strictly larger than the Action SPOF.
wrenches.mitigation: Make the protocol transport-AGNOSTIC: frames/echos are already content-addressed and signed, so identity is the KEY, not the URL. Mirror to a second host (a second git host, S3, IPFS) and have edges try an ordered list of gateways. Publish a signed 'transport directory' the swarm can update so edges can fail over to a new home. Keep nothing in the protocol that depends on a specific GitHub URL shape.
wrenches.residual_risk: Failover requires edges to already hold the alternate-gateway list before the primary dies; an edge in a long blackout when the repo is taken down and the directory updated may never learn the new home until it surfaces.

wrenches.title: Economic / rate-limit DoS of the substrate as a governance-grade silencing weapon
wrenches.scenario: The foundry is a metered GitHub Action; Issues API and the raw CDN have rate limits and abuse detection. An attacker — or a sybil flood — can deliberately exhaust the org's Action minutes, trip secondary rate limits, or trigger GitHub's abuse-flagging, starving the WHOLE swarm of echo updates at near-zero cost to the attacker, and potentially getting the org auto-suspended — turning GitHub's own abuse defense into the weapon. Whoever can exhaust the budget can silence the swarm.
wrenches.impact: Planetary DoS of guidance updates and possible org suspension, achievable cheaply and asymmetrically. Governance-relevant: budget exhaustion equals control over whether the swarm is heard.
wrenches.mitigation: Per-edge quotas and admission control BEFORE the foundry spends a cent. Batch/debounce foundry runs (never run-per-telemetry-item); cap fan-out. Monitor spend and degrade gracefully — edges already keep running on last echo, so make substrate-DoS an explicit, tested fallback. Multi-substrate (so one provider's limit isn't the heartbeat). Reserve a paid, isolated runner pool for the signing/authority step so free-tier abuse cannot starve authority.
wrenches.residual_risk: Quotas can themselves be gamed by sybil identities; a well-funded attacker can sustain pressure across multiple substrates, and graceful degradation still means stale guidance during the attack.

wrenches.title: Long-lived immutable signed objects + a quantum future = harvest-now, forge-later: today's Ed25519/ECDSA signatures on permanent interplanetary artifacts become forgeable within their own lifetime
wrenches.scenario: The crypto roadmap is HMAC -> Ed25519 -> ECDSA with sig-suite agility, and the whole design prizes PERMANENCE: content-addressed, append-only, immutable, signed objects meant to remain verifiable for the multi-year-to-decade life of an interplanetary edge or an archived .egg/frame chain. Ed25519 and ECDSA are both broken by a cryptographically-relevant quantum computer (Shor). Because the signed objects are public, immutable, and long-lived, an adversary harvests them now and, once quantum capability arrives within the artifact's lifetime, forges new objects that chain validly onto the old (signed) history — including re-anchoring canon, minting echos for still-deployed edges that pinned the now-broken keys, and back-dating a rewritten 'append-only' chain that verifies. An offline edge launched today with a classical verify key baked in cannot be told its root is broken until the next contact window, which at interplanetary scale may be after the break.
wrenches.impact: Every signature the system relies on for its core 'verify-before-act / immutable canon / signed chain' value has a finite cryptographic shelf life that is SHORTER than the advertised permanence of the artifacts it protects. Long-lived edges become forgeable mid-mission; the immutable audit chain becomes retroactively rewritable; the trust root that was supposed to be eternal has an expiry the design never planned for.
wrenches.mitigation: Bake crypto-agility into the OBJECT FORMAT, not just the roadmap: every signed object carries (sig-suite-id, key-id) so the verifier can require a minimum suite and reject deprecated ones. Plan a post-quantum migration path now (hybrid classical+PQC signatures — e.g., Ed25519 + ML-DSA/Dilithium — so an object stays valid if EITHER holds during transition). For artifacts that must outlive their crypto, support re-signing/re-anchoring with a fresh suite before the old one is broken, and timestamp-anchor the chain so the order of signing is independently provable even if a key later breaks. Pre-provision edges with upgradable verify roots, not a single baked-in classical key.
wrenches.residual_risk: PQC signatures are larger/slower — costly on the constrained, bandwidth-starved edges that are the design's whole point; re-signing immutable history is itself a delicate, attackable ceremony; an edge already launched with only-classical roots and no upgrade path is simply exposed once the break lands within its mission window.
